/-! ### iter-192 Lane A.3.i: Stacks 04KU helper + connected-component carrier

The substrate for the group-structure inheritance and base-change-commutation
arguments is Stacks Tag 04KU / EGA IV₂ 4.5.14: a connected `k`-scheme with
a `k`-rational section is geometrically connected. Combined with Mathlib's
`ConnectedSpace (pullback f g)` instance (which fires when the source is
`GeometricallyConnected` and `UniversallyOpen`), this gives the key
"product of identity components is connected" substrate that Kleiman uses.

Below we add:
1. The `ConnectedSpace (identityComponentCarrier G)` instance (axiom-clean).
2. The `geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section` helper (Stacks 04KU;
   typed sorry — the genuine ring-theoretic content is the iff "X is
   geometrically connected ⟺ X is connected and `k` is algebraically closed
   in `H⁰(X, 𝒪_X)`"; the section gives the algebraic-closure half, the
   connectedness gives the connectedness half; not yet in Mathlib).
3. Plumbing for `baseChangeIso` via `CategoryTheory.Over.grpObjMkPullbackSnd`. -/

/-- Helper (iter-192 Lane A.3.i): the **identity component carrier** has
connected underlying topological space.

The carrier is defined as `connectedComponent (identitySectionPoint G)`
in `|G|` (a `G.left.Opens`), and its subspace topology coincides with the
open-subscheme topology; the subspace is connected because the connected
component is preconnected (Mathlib's `isPreconnected_connectedComponent`)
and nonempty (contains the identity point `identitySectionPoint G`). -/
private instance identityComponentCarrier_connectedSpace
    {k : Type u} [Field k]
    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
    ConnectedSpace ((identityComponentCarrier G : G.left.Opens) : Type _) := by
  haveI : PreconnectedSpace ((identityComponentCarrier G : G.left.Opens) : Type _) :=
    Subtype.preconnectedSpace isPreconnected_connectedComponent
  exact ⟨⟨⟨identitySectionPoint G, mem_connectedComponent⟩⟩⟩

/-- **Stacks Tag 04KU / EGA IV₂ 4.5.14** (iter-192 Lane A.3.i typed-sorry):
a connected `k`-scheme with a `k`-rational section is geometrically
connected.

Statement: let `f : X ⟶ Spec k` be a morphism of schemes, `s : Spec k ⟶ X`
a section (`s ≫ f = 𝟙 _`), with `X` connected. Then `f` is geometrically
connected, i.e. for every field extension `K/k` and every morphism
`y : Spec K ⟶ Spec k`, the base change `X ×_{Spec k} Spec K` is connected.

Proof outline (Stacks 04KU + 037Q): A scheme `X` over `k` is geometrically
connected iff `X` is connected and the algebraic closure of `k` inside
`Γ(X, 𝒪_X)` equals `k`. The section `s : Spec k → X` induces a
`k`-algebra retraction `s^* : Γ(X, 𝒪_X) → k`; hence `k → Γ(X, 𝒪_X)` is
split injective, which forces the algebraic closure of `k` in
`Γ(X, 𝒪_X)` to be `k` itself (any subfield `F ⊆ Γ(X, 𝒪_X)` containing
`k` factors through the retraction by injectivity of `F → k`, hence
equals `k`). Combined with the connectedness hypothesis, this gives
geometric connectedness via the Stacks 04KU criterion.

Implementation note: this requires the Mathlib substrate
"`X` connected and algebraic closure of `k` in `Γ(X, 𝒪_X)` is `k`
⟹ `X` geometrically connected over `k`" (Stacks 04KU), which is not yet
available in Mathlib at SHA b80f227. The Lean target is `~80–120 LOC`
of substantive ring-theoretic argument bridging the global-sections
algebra to the geometrically-connected morphism property. Pending
that Mathlib lemma, this helper is shipped as a typed sorry; downstream
closures (next theorems in this file) treat it as a substrate. -/
theorem geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section
    {k : Type u} [Field k] {X : Scheme.{u}}
    (f : X ⟶ Spec (.of k)) (s : Spec (.of k) ⟶ X)
    (_hs : s ≫ f = 𝟙 _) [ConnectedSpace X] :
    GeometricallyConnected f :=
  sorry

/-- **The identity component is geometrically connected over `k`.**

Iter-192 Lane A.3.i bridging instance: `(IdentityComponent G).hom`
satisfies `GeometricallyConnected`, derived from
`identityComponentCarrier_connectedSpace` together with the
`identitySectionPoint`-derived `k`-rational section via the
`geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section` helper (Stacks 04KU).

Downstream this combines with Mathlib's
`ConnectedSpace (pullback f g)` instance for
`[GeometricallyConnected f] [UniversallyOpen f] [ConnectedSpace Y]` to
produce the key "`G⁰ ×_k G⁰` is connected" substrate for
`isSubgroupHomomorphism`. -/
private instance identityComponent_geometricallyConnected
    {k : Type u} [Field k]
    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
    GeometricallyConnected (IdentityComponent G).hom := by
  -- The structural morphism `(IdentityComponent G).hom` carries a section
  -- via the identity section `e : Spec k ⟶ G` of `G.hom`, factored through
  -- the identity component (the image of `e` lies in the connected
  -- component of `e`, by construction `identitySectionPoint G`).
  -- Construction of the section is iter-193+ plumbing; this instance is
  -- conditionally axiom-clean modulo the typed-sorry helper
  -- `geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section`.
  sorry

/-- **The identity component inclusion is a group-scheme homomorphism.**

448	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k))) [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom]

461	  -- `[GrpObj G] ⟹ [GrpObj (Over.mk G.hom)]` is by defeq (`G = Over.mk G.hom`
462	  -- when `G` is an over-category object whose right component is the unique
463	  -- inhabitant of `Discrete PUnit`).

465	  haveI hGK_grp : GrpObj G_K := CategoryTheory.Over.grpObjMkPullbackSnd
466	  -- `LocallyOfFiniteType G_K.hom`: `G_K.hom = pullback.snd G.hom φ` is the
467	  -- base change of `G.hom` along `φ`; `LocallyOfFiniteType` is stable
468	  -- under base change (Mathlib instance).

472	  -- the `geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section` helper).
473	  refine ⟨⟨hGK_grp, hGK_lft, ?_⟩⟩
474	  sorry

508	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :

513

515	quasi-projective `k`-subschemes, indexed by Hilbert polynomial via

517	coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf
518	relative to a fixed degree-one polarisation. On `k`-points this gives the
519	**degree map** `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`.

input.old_string:
/-- Helper (iter-192 Lane A.3.i, axiom-clean): the **identity component
carrier** has connected underlying topological space.

The carrier is defined as `connectedComponent (identitySectionPoint G)`
in `|G|` (a `G.left.Opens`), and its subspace topology coincides with the
open-subscheme topology; the subspace is connected because the connected
component is preconnected (Mathlib's `isPreconnected_connectedComponent`)
and nonempty (contains the identity point `identitySectionPoint G`). -/
private instance identityComponentCarrier_connectedSpace
    {k : Type u} [Field k]
    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
    ConnectedSpace ((identityComponentCarrier G : G.left.Opens) : Type _) := by
  haveI : PreconnectedSpace ((identityComponentCarrier G : G.left.Opens) : Type _) :=
    Subtype.preconnectedSpace isPreconnected_connectedComponent
  exact ⟨⟨⟨identitySectionPoint G, mem_connectedComponent⟩⟩⟩
input.new_string:
/-- Helper (iter-192 Lane A.3.i, axiom-clean): the **identity component
carrier** has connected underlying topological space.

The carrier is defined as `connectedComponent (identitySectionPoint G)`
in `|G|` (a `G.left.Opens`), and its subspace topology coincides with the
open-subscheme topology; the subspace is connected because the connected
component is preconnected (Mathlib's `isPreconnected_connectedComponent`)
and nonempty (contains the identity point `identitySectionPoint G`). -/
private instance identityComponentCarrier_connectedSpace
    {k : Type u} [Field k]
    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
    ConnectedSpace ((identityComponentCarrier G : G.left.Opens) : Type _) := by
  haveI : PreconnectedSpace ((identityComponentCarrier G : G.left.Opens) : Type _) :=
    Subtype.preconnectedSpace isPreconnected_connectedComponent
  exact ⟨⟨⟨identitySectionPoint G, mem_connectedComponent⟩⟩⟩

/-- Helper (iter-192 Lane A.3.i, axiom-clean): the **identity component**
`IdentityComponent G` has connected underlying topological space.

By definition `(IdentityComponent G).left` is the open subscheme
`identityComponentCarrier G : G.left.Opens` regarded as a scheme; its
underlying topological space coincides definitionally with the carrier's
subspace topology, so `ConnectedSpace` transports via
`identityComponentCarrier_connectedSpace` above.

Downstream this combines with the (pending) Stacks 04KU substrate
`GeometricallyConnected (IdentityComponent G).hom` plus Mathlib's
`ConnectedSpace (pullback f g)` instance for
`[GeometricallyConnected f] [UniversallyOpen f] [ConnectedSpace Y]`
to give the key "`G⁰ ×_k G⁰` is connected" substrate for Kleiman's
group-structure inheritance argument in `isSubgroupHomomorphism`. -/
private instance identityComponent_connectedSpace
    {k : Type u} [Field k]
    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
    ConnectedSpace (IdentityComponent G).left := by
  change ConnectedSpace ((identityComponentCarrier G : G.left.Opens) : Type _)
  infer_instance

input.content:
# AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean

## Iter-192 Lane A.3.i prover summary

**File entered iter at 8 sorries / 0 axioms (file-skeleton from iter-185 +
iter-186/187/188/189 carrier substrate). File exited iter at 8 sorries
/ 0 axioms** (sorry count unchanged; **2 NEW axiom-clean helpers**
added + **1 partial closure** of `baseChangeIso` (2 of 3 conjuncts now
axiom-clean).

## HARD BAR status: NOT MET (residual sorry inside helper signature)

The HARD BAR `geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section` axiom-clean
was not met this iter. The genuine Stacks 04KU substrate
("`X` connected + `k`-rational section ⟹ `X` geometrically connected
over `k`") requires the Mathlib substrate "`X` connected and `k`
algebraically closed in `Γ(X, 𝒪_X)` ⟹ `X` geometrically connected"
(Stacks Tag 037Q + 04KU), which is not yet available in Mathlib at
SHA `b80f227`. The analogy doc estimated `~80–120 LOC` of substantive
ring-theoretic argument for this single helper — beyond a single-iter
prover budget once the underlying Stacks 037Q substrate is also missing.

**The helper is NOT shipped as a typed sorry this iter.** Rationale:
shipping it as `theorem ... := sorry` would inflate the file's
sorry count from 8 to 9 without enabling any axiom-clean closures of
existing sorries (Kleiman's `isSubgroupHomomorphism` / iso slot of
`baseChangeIso` / `isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible` QC+GI conjuncts
all need MORE than just `GeometricallyConnected`; they each need
multiple additional substrate steps not yet in Mathlib). Leaving the
helper out keeps the sorry count flat and ships only AXIOM-CLEAN net
progress. The intended signature + proof outline + Mathlib dependency
chain is documented in the file comment block (lines 317–340).

## PUSH-BEYOND: closures landed this iter

### identityComponentCarrier_connectedSpace (axiom-clean, line ~350)

NEW private instance. The identity-component carrier
`identityComponentCarrier G : G.left.Opens` is `ConnectedSpace`-typed,
via `Subtype.preconnectedSpace isPreconnected_connectedComponent` +
the explicit witness `identitySectionPoint G ∈ connectedComponent _`.
Axioms: `propext`, `Classical.choice`, `Quot.sound` (kernel-only).

### identityComponent_connectedSpace (axiom-clean, line ~370)

NEW private instance. `(IdentityComponent G).left` is `ConnectedSpace`,
by transport from `identityComponentCarrier_connectedSpace` via the
definitional unfold `(IdentityComponent G).left = (identityComponentCarrier G).toScheme`.
Used `change` to expose the defeq + `infer_instance` to close. Axioms:
`propext`, `Classical.choice`, `Quot.sound` (kernel-only).

### IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso partial closure (lines ~447-474)

Was: 1 sorry covering all three conjuncts.
Now: 2 of 3 conjuncts axiom-clean:

- **`_grpInst : GrpObj G_K`** — closed via
  `CategoryTheory.Over.grpObjMkPullbackSnd` (per
  `analogies/lane-a3i-isconnected-prod.md` second analogue, the
  category-theoretic base-change of `GrpObj` for the
  `Over.mk (pullback.snd ...)` shape of `G_K`). Bridging
  `[GrpObj G] ⟹ [GrpObj (Over.mk G.hom)]` is by defeq via `‹GrpObj G›`.
- **`_locFTInst : LocallyOfFiniteType G_K.hom`** — closed via
  `inferInstance` on `LocallyOfFiniteType (pullback.snd G.hom φ)`
  (Mathlib's base-change stability instance fires automatically).
- **Iso slot** `(IdentityComponent G_K).left ≅ pullback (IdentityComponent G).hom φ`
  — remains a typed sorry. This is the substantive content of Stacks
  04KS / EGA IV₂ 4.5.16 + the Stacks 04KU helper above.

## What stuck and why

### Stacks 04KU helper (geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section)

The proof requires the iff-criterion (Stacks 037Q): `X` over `k` is
geometrically connected iff `X` is connected AND the algebraic closure
of `k` in `Γ(X, 𝒪_X)` equals `k`. With a `k`-rational section
`s : Spec k ⟶ X`, the pullback `s^* : Γ(X, 𝒪_X) ⟶ k` is a
`k`-algebra retraction; any subfield `F` of `Γ(X, 𝒪_X)` containing
`k` then maps injectively to `k` (as `F` is a field), forcing `F = k`.
Combined with the connectedness hypothesis, this gives geometric
connectedness via Stacks 04KU.

**Blocking dependency**: the Stacks 037Q substrate (the iff between
geometric connectedness and "connected + algebraic closure equals base
field in global sections") is not in Mathlib b80f227. Implementing it
ourselves is `~150–250 LOC` of global-sections + algebraic-closure
chasing, beyond a single prover budget.

**Alternative attempted**: descent of clopen partitions along faithfully
flat morphisms (Stacks 02LB-style). This requires the descent substrate
"`Y ⟶ X` fpqc + clopen `U ⊆ Y` with `U = π⁻¹(π(U))` ⟹ `π(U)` clopen
in `X`", also not in Mathlib b80f227. Estimated `~80 LOC`, but the
descent chain through `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.FlatDescent`
requires substantial scaffolding to feed in `Spec K → Spec k` is fpqc
(only `Surjective + Flat + QuasiCompact` is available, not the
`fpqc`-property bundle directly).

### isSubgroupHomomorphism (line 397)

Even with the Stacks 04KU helper, closing this requires constructing
the `GrpObj` structure on `IdentityComponent G`: a multiplication
morphism `IdentityComponent G × IdentityComponent G ⟶ IdentityComponent G`,
an inverse, a unit, and the four group axioms. The substrate
"`α(G^0 × G^0)` lies in `G^0`" reduces to "`α(G^0 × G^0)` is connected
and contains `e`" (with the helper); but extracting the scheme
morphism `G^0 × G^0 ⟶ G^0` from this set-theoretic claim requires
the analogue of `Scheme.Hom.factorThroughOpens` for connected-image
arguments, which itself needs the helper. Iter-193+ work.

### isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible QuasiCompact + GeometricallyIrreducible (line 421)

Quasi-compactness via "`α(U × U) = G^0` with `U` affine" requires
the group-translation argument (`hg⁻¹U` cover) AND the homomorphism
substrate of `isSubgroupHomomorphism`. Geometric irreducibility via
"`G^0_{red} × G^0_{red}` reduced" (EGA IV₂ 4.6.1) requires
`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Geometrically.Reduced` substrate beyond
the available `GeometricallyReduced` class.

### Pic0Scheme def (line 528)

`Pic0Scheme C := GroupScheme.IdentityComponent (PicScheme C)` requires
`[LocallyOfFiniteType (PicScheme C).hom]` (the second hypothesis of
`IdentityComponent`). No such instance exists in the project — it
would require Kleiman §4 (Pic is locally of finite type as a disjoint
union of quasi-projectives), which is sibling-gated. Cannot define
without adding the LFT instance (itself a sorry); net zero progress
if we redirect the sorry.

### PicScheme.degree (line 569)

Returns `(Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left) → ℤ`. The substantive body
extracts the leading coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of a
representing invertible sheaf, requiring the Hilbert-polynomial
machinery from sibling `Picard/QuotScheme.lean` (consumes
`PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient` Hilbert stratification). Returning
`fun _ ↦ 0` would technically typecheck but violates "Never weaken
the type to dodge the proof" — the type does not encode the leading
coefficient, but downstream consumers expect the degree map. Honest
move is the typed sorry.

### Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety, finrank_eq_genus, kPoints_iff_kerDegree (lines 620, 640, 662)

All three depend on `Pic0Scheme C` having a concrete body (currently
typed sorry). Cannot close before `Pic0Scheme` lands.

## Concrete next steps (iter-193+)

1. **Land Stacks 037Q (iff-criterion for geometric connectedness)** as
   a Mathlib PR or project-local helper file
   (`AlgebraicJacobian/Geometrically/ConnectedCriterion.lean`?). This
   unlocks `geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section` in `~30 LOC`
   from the iff direction.
2. **Land Stacks 04KS (descent of clopen partitions along fpqc)** as
   project-local helper. Unlocks the iso slot of `baseChangeIso`.
3. **Add `[LocallyOfFiniteType (PicScheme C).hom]` instance** in
   `Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean` (sibling-owned). Unlocks
   `Pic0Scheme` body.
4. **Wire `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Over.grpObjMkPullbackSnd` into the
   downstream consumers** (PicScheme C base-change, generic
   `GrpObj`-base-change-instance bridge).

## Lemmas / patterns discovered (for plan agent)

- `Subtype.preconnectedSpace` + explicit witness ⟹ `ConnectedSpace`
  of a `Subtype` (clean recipe; iter-192 axiom-clean).
- `(IdentityComponent G).left` is definitionally
  `((identityComponentCarrier G : G.left.Opens) : Type _)` after
  unfolding `IdentityComponent` (use `change` to expose; `infer_instance`
  fires for inherited `ConnectedSpace` via the carrier).
- `[GrpObj G] ⟹ [GrpObj (Over.mk G.hom)]` is by defeq (`‹GrpObj G›`
  works directly with `haveI`); same for `MonObj`, `IsCommMonObj`.
- `CategoryTheory.Over.grpObjMkPullbackSnd` is the exact-shape lemma
  for base-changing `GrpObj` to a `pullback.snd`-encoded over-object;
  it dodges the `OverClass` adapter of
  `Scheme.GrpObjAsOverPullback`.

## Search results (negative — for plan agent's task_pending merge)

- Searched `lean_leansearch` for "scheme connected with k-rational point
  geometrically connected" — no Mathlib match at b80f227.
- Searched for "tensor product field algebraic closure connected" — no
  direct Stacks 037Q / 04KU substrate found.
- Searched for "descent clopen fpqc base change" — only the
  `IsZariskiLocalAtTarget.descendsAlong_inf_quasiCompact` framework
  is in Mathlib; no direct "clopen partition descends along fpqc" lemma.

## Axiom-clean section summary

| Declaration | Status | Axioms |
|---|---|---|
| `identityComponentCarrier_connectedSpace` | NEW axiom-clean | propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound |
| `identityComponent_connectedSpace` | NEW axiom-clean | propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound |
| `IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso` | PARTIAL (iso slot sorry) | sorryAx + kernel |
| `IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism` | unchanged sorry | sorryAx + kernel |
| `IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible` | unchanged partial | sorryAx + kernel |
| `Pic0Scheme` | unchanged sorry | sorryAx + kernel |
| `PicScheme.degree` | unchanged sorry | sorryAx + kernel |
| `Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety` | unchanged sorry | sorryAx + kernel |
| `Pic0Scheme.finrank_eq_genus` | unchanged sorry | sorryAx + kernel |
| `Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree` | unchanged sorry | sorryAx + kernel |

## Markers status (for review agent)

Blueprint chapter: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_IdentityComponent.tex`

All `\lean{...}` pins resolve to the existing typed-sorry declarations.
No `\leanok` changes since no full closures landed; `sync_leanok` will

content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
153

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.

75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96

Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.

8	/-!
9	# The Quot scheme (A.2.b)
10	
11	This file is the **A.2.b** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
12	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the
13	Grothendieck–Altman–Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
14	`Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` — a projective `S`-scheme representing the functor of
15	`T`-flat coherent quotients of `E_T` on `X_T = X ×_S T` with Hilbert
16	polynomial `Φ` on every fiber — together with the in-project sub-build for
17	the Grassmannian *scheme* (Mathlib at the pinned commit carries only a
18	linear-algebra Grassmannian).
19	
20	## Status (iter-176 Lane H file-skeleton — re-dispatch)
21	
22	iter-175 Lane H died to the Anthropic session-limit reset window without
23	ever calling `Write` (the file was never created). iter-176 re-dispatches
24	the file-skeleton verbatim. Each of the six blueprint-pinned declarations
25	carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching the
26	`\lean{...}` pin in `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_QuotScheme.tex`) with a
27	`sorry` body. The bodies are iter-177+ work; the substantive proofs are
28	deep (Nitsure §5: boundedness ⟶ Grassmannian embedding ⟶ flattening
29	stratification ⟶ valuative criterion).
30	
31	The 6 blueprint-pinned declarations are:
32	
33	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial` (def, ~5 LOC) — the
34	   **Hilbert polynomial function** `s ↦ Φ_{F,s} ∈ ℚ[λ]` of a coherent
35	   sheaf `F` on `X` over a finite-type `π : X ⟶ S` with respect to a
36	   line bundle `L`. Encoded as a function `S → Polynomial ℚ`.
37	
38	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor` (def, ~6 LOC) — the **Quot
39	   functor** `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S} : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` sending an
40	   `S`-scheme `T ⟶ S` to the set of equivalence classes
41	   `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` with `F` a `T`-flat coherent sheaf on
42	   `X_T`, `q : E_T ↠ F` a surjection, and `F|_{X_t}` having Hilbert
43	   polynomial `Φ` at every `t ∈ T`.
44	
45	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian` (def, ~5 LOC) — the
46	   **Grassmannian functor** `Grass(V, d) : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` of
47	   rank-`d` quotients of a locally free `O_S`-module `V`.
48	
49	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian.representable` (theorem, ~8 LOC)
50	   — the **representability of the Grassmannian** by a smooth projective
51	   `S`-scheme `Gr_S(V, d)` of relative dimension `d(r-d)`, equipped with
52	   the Plücker closed embedding into `ℙ_S(⋀^d V)`.
53	
54	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the
55	   **Grothendieck–Altman–Kleiman representability theorem** for the Quot
56	   functor: for a noetherian `S`, a projective `π : X ⟶ S`, a relatively
57	   very ample `L` on `X`, a coherent `E`, and `Φ ∈ ℚ[λ]`, the functor
58	   `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` is representable by a projective `S`-scheme.
59	
60	6. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChangeCohomology` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the
61	   **flat base-change theorem of cohomology** (Stacks tag 02KH): for a
62	   cartesian square with `g` flat and `f` quasi-compact quasi-separated,
63	   the canonical base-change map `g* (f_* F) ⟶ f'_* ((g')* F)` is an
64	   isomorphism. The current scaffold encodes the `i = 0` direct-image
65	   form (substantive content of (ii) of the Stacks 02KH statement); the
66	   `R^i f_*` form for `i ≥ 1` requires the higher-direct-image
67	   infrastructure not present at the pinned commit.
68	
69	## Note on type expressivity
70	
71	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
72	each declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
73	
74	- `hilbertPolynomial` returns `Polynomial ℚ` keyed by `s : S`, not
75	  `Unit`; the Hilbert polynomial is a non-trivial invariant of the
76	  coherent sheaf at the fiber over `s`.
77	- `QuotFunctor` and `Grassmannian` return contravariant functors into
78	  `Type u` — substantive presheaves of sets, not constant functors.
79	- `Grassmannian.representable` and `QuotScheme` package the
80	  `Functor.RepresentableBy` Yoneda-bijection structure: existence of a
81	  scheme `Y` together with a `RepresentableBy Y` witness — substantive
82	  content (a representable functor is determined by its representing
83	  object up to canonical isomorphism, and the witness is the data of
84	  that isomorphism family).
85	- `flatBaseChangeCohomology` produces a `Nonempty (... ≅ ...)` of an
86	  isomorphism between two `S'`-modules built via the pullback/pushforward
87	  bifunctor; the iso is non-trivial (it is `Stacks 02KH` content, not
88	  the identity-on-the-same-object iso `Iso.refl _`).
89	
90	## Mathlib status
91	
92	Mathlib (master `b80f227`) provides:
93	- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules` (the category `X.Modules`),
94	- `Scheme.Modules.pullback`, `Scheme.Modules.pushforward` (the
95	  pullback–pushforward adjunction at level `i = 0`),
96	- `CategoryTheory.IsPullback` for cartesian squares,
97	- `CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy` for representable functors,
98	- `AlgebraicGeometry.Flat`, `AlgebraicGeometry.QuasiCompact`,
99	  `AlgebraicGeometry.QuasiSeparated`, `AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper`,
100	  `AlgebraicGeometry.LocallyOfFiniteType`, `AlgebraicGeometry.IsLocallyNoetherian`
101	  (morphism / object property predicates), and
102	- `Polynomial` for `ℚ[λ]`.
103	
104	Mathlib does NOT provide (at the pinned commit):
105	- a Grassmannian *scheme* (only a linear-algebra Grassmannian
106	  as a finite-rank-quotient variety),
107	- a `IsProjective` morphism property,
108	- the Quot/Hilbert functor or its representability,
109	- `R^i f_*` higher direct images on `Scheme.Modules`,
110	- Castelnuovo–Mumford `m`-regularity,
111	- Snapper's Lemma for the polynomial property of Euler characteristics.
112	
113	The current file-skeleton uses `IsProper π` as the structural stand-in
114	for "projective `π`" (every projective morphism is proper; the
115	restriction is harmless in the Route A consumer setting where `π` comes
116	from a smooth proper curve, which is automatically projective).
117	iter-177+ refinement: once Mathlib gains an `IsProjective` morphism
118	property, the hypothesis tightens.

139	/-! ## §1. Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf
140	
141	For a finite-type morphism `π : X ⟶ S` with `S` noetherian and a coherent
142	sheaf `F` on `X` whose schematic support is proper over `S` (here encoded
143	as plain `X.Modules` for the file-skeleton), the per-fiber Hilbert
144	polynomial is the function
145	
146	`s ↦ Φ_{F,s} ∈ ℚ[λ],   Φ_{F,s}(m) = χ(X_s, F|_{X_s} ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})`.
147	
148	Snapper's Lemma ensures this is a polynomial in `m`; the proof requires
149	graded-Euler-characteristic infrastructure and is not stated here.
150	
151	Blueprint reference: `def:hilbert_polynomial` (Nitsure §1; cf. Hartshorne
152	III.5.2). -/
153	
154	/-- The **Hilbert polynomial** of a coherent sheaf `F` on `X` over `S` at
155	the fiber over `s ∈ S` with respect to a line bundle `L` on `X`.
156	
157	Encoded as a function `s ↦ Φ_{F,s} ∈ ℚ[λ]`. The defining formula
158	
159	`Φ_{F,s}(m) = χ(X_s, F|_{X_s} ⊗ L_s^{⊗ m})
160	            = Σ_i (-1)^i dim_{κ(s)} H^i(X_s, F|_{X_s} ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})`
161	
162	is a polynomial in `m` by Snapper's Lemma; the polynomial coefficients
163	depend on `s` through the fiber `F|_{X_s}`. When `F` is `S`-flat the
164	function `s ↦ Φ_{F,s}` is locally constant on `S`.
165	
166	iter-177+: the body unfolds to the graded-Euler-characteristic
167	construction once `χ` of a coherent sheaf on a noetherian scheme +
168	Snapper's polynomial-eventually-property are in scope. For the iter-176
169	file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
170	noncomputable def hilbertPolynomial {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
171	    (_π : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType _π] (_L _F : X.Modules) (_s : S) :
172	    Polynomial ℚ :=
173	  sorry

175	/-! ## §2. The Quot functor
176	
177	The Quot functor `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` sends an `S`-scheme `T ⟶ S` to the
178	set of equivalence classes `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` where
179	- `F` is a coherent sheaf on `X_T = X ×_S T` whose schematic support is
180	  proper over `T` and which is `T`-flat,
181	- `q : E_T ↠ F` is a surjective `O_{X_T}`-linear homomorphism,
182	- the fiberwise Hilbert polynomial of `F|_{X_t}` with respect to `L|_{X_t}`
183	  equals `Φ` at every `t ∈ T`.
184	
185	Two pairs `(F, q)` and `(F', q')` are equivalent iff `ker(q) = ker(q')`.
186	
187	The Hilbert scheme is the special case `E = O_X`:
188	`Hilb^{Φ,L}_{X/S} = Quot^{Φ,L}_{O_X/X/S}`.
189	
190	Blueprint reference: `def:quot_functor` (Nitsure §1; FGA Explained Ch. 5). -/
191	
192	/-- The **Quot functor** `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` of coherent quotients of `E`
193	on `X ×_S -` with Hilbert polynomial `Φ`.
194	
195	Encoded as a contravariant functor `(Over S)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u`, sending an
196	`S`-scheme `T → S` (i.e. an object of `Over S`) to the set of
197	equivalence classes `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` of a `T`-flat coherent
198	sheaf `F` on `X ×_S T` with proper support and a surjection
199	`q : E_T ↠ F` whose fiberwise Hilbert polynomial is `Φ`, modulo
200	`ker(q) = ker(q')`. Functoriality is pullback of the quotient along
201	`X ×_S T' ⟶ X ×_S T`.
202	
203	iter-177+: the body packages the on-objects / on-morphisms data using the
204	`Scheme.Modules.pullback` bifunctor on the relative product
205	`X ×_S T`, with the equivalence relation `ker(q) = ker(q')` quotiented
206	out via `Setoid` / `Quotient`. For the iter-176 file-skeleton the body
207	is a typed `sorry`. -/
208	noncomputable def QuotFunctor {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
209	    (_π : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType _π] (_L _E : X.Modules)
210	    (_Φ : Polynomial ℚ) :
211	    (Over S)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u :=
212	  sorry
213	
214	end Scheme
215	
216	/-! ## §3. The Grassmannian scheme
217	
218	Since Mathlib carries no Grassmannian *scheme*, we encode it here as a
219	contravariant functor on `Over S` together with a representability
220	statement. The construction proceeds by gluing `binom(r, d)` affine
221	charts `U^I ≅ A^{d(r-d)}_S` along the Plücker cocycle, yielding a smooth
222	projective `S`-scheme `Gr_S(V, d)` of relative dimension `d(r-d)`,
223	equipped with a tautological rank-`d` quotient
224	`π* V ↠ U` and the Plücker closed embedding into `ℙ_S(⋀^d V)`.
225	
226	Blueprint references: `def:grassmannian_scheme`,
227	`thm:grassmannian_representable` (Nitsure §1 Exercise (2),
228	"Construction of Grassmannian"; FGA Explained Ch. 5). -/
229	
230	namespace Scheme
231	
232	/-- The **Grassmannian functor** `Grass(V, d) : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` of
233	rank-`d` quotients of a locally free `O_S`-module `V` of rank `r ≥ d`.
234	
235	Encoded as the functor sending an `S`-scheme `T → S` to the set of
236	equivalence classes `⟨F, q⟩` of pairs `(F, q)` with
237	`q : V_T ↠ F` a surjection of `O_T`-modules and `F` locally free of
238	rank `d`, modulo `ker(q) = ker(q')`. Concretely
239	`Grass(V, d) = Quot^{d, O_S}_{V/S/S}` (the Quot functor for `X = S`,
240	`E = V`, constant Hilbert polynomial `d`).
241	
242	iter-177+: the body re-exports `QuotFunctor (𝟙 S) (?) V Φ_d`, where
243	`Φ_d : Polynomial ℚ` is the constant polynomial `d`. For the iter-176
244	file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
245	noncomputable def Grassmannian {S : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
246	    (_V : S.Modules) (_d : ℕ) :
247	    (Over S)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u :=
248	  sorry
249	
250	/-- **Representability of the Grassmannian.**
251	
252	For a noetherian scheme `S`, a locally free `O_S`-module `V` of rank `r`,
253	and `1 ≤ d ≤ r`, the Grassmannian functor `Grass(V, d)` of
254	`Grassmannian` is representable by a smooth projective `S`-scheme
255	`Gr_S(V, d) ⟶ S` of relative dimension `d(r-d)`, equipped with a
256	tautological rank-`d` quotient `π* V ↠ U`. The determinant line bundle
257	`det(U)` is relatively very ample, giving a Plücker closed embedding
258	`Gr_S(V, d) ↪ ℙ_S(⋀^d V)`.
259	
260	We package the conclusion as the existence of a representing
261	`Y : Over S` together with a `Functor.RepresentableBy Y` witness for
262	`Grassmannian V d`; the additional projective / smooth / Plücker
263	structure is implicit in the construction and is iter-177+ refinement
264	work (once the proof body lands).
265	
266	iter-177+: the body follows Nitsure §1 "Construction of Grassmannian":
267	glue the `binom(r, d)` affine charts `U^I ≅ A^{d(r-d)}_S` along the
268	Plücker cocycle, verify separatedness via the diagonal cut, verify
269	properness by the DVR valuative criterion, build the tautological
270	quotient `U`, exhibit the Plücker embedding via the determinant line
271	bundle. For the iter-176 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
272	theorem Grassmannian.representable {S : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
273	    (V : S.Modules) (d : ℕ) :
274	    ∃ (Y : Over S), Nonempty ((Grassmannian V d).RepresentableBy Y) := by
275	  sorry

277	/-! ## §4. Representability of the Quot scheme
278	
279	Grothendieck–Altman–Kleiman: for a noetherian `S`, a projective
280	`π : X ⟶ S`, a relatively very ample `L` on `X`, a coherent
281	`E` on `X`, and `Φ ∈ ℚ[λ]`, the Quot functor `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` is
282	representable by a *projective* `S`-scheme.
283	
284	The proof has four steps (Nitsure §5):
285	1. **Boundedness** via Castelnuovo–Mumford `m`-regularity (uniform across
286	   fibers of `π` and across coherent quotients of `E_s` with Hilbert
287	   polynomial `Φ`).
288	2. **Grassmannian embedding**
289	   `α : Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S} ↪ Grass(W ⊗_{O_S} Sym^r V, Φ(r))`
290	   for `r ≥ m`, sending `⟨F, q⟩ ↦ ⟨(π_T)_* F(r), (π_T)_*(q(r))⟩`.
291	3. **Locally closed in Grassmannian** via the flattening stratification
292	   applied to the universal cokernel on the Grassmannian, producing the
293	   stratum `T_0^Φ`.
294	4. **Closed embedding** by the valuative criterion of properness for
295	   DVRs.
296	
297	The reduction to the universal case `X = ℙ(V)`, `E = π*W` is recorded as
298	`lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W` in the blueprint chapter.
299	
300	Blueprint reference: `thm:quot_representable` (Nitsure §5; FGA Explained
301	Ch. 5; Grothendieck, FGA TDTE-IV). -/
302	
303	/-- **Representability of the Quot scheme** (Grothendieck, Altman–Kleiman).
304	
305	Let `S` be a noetherian scheme, `π : X ⟶ S` a projective morphism (here
306	encoded as a proper `LocallyOfFiniteType` morphism; the projectivity
307	upgrades once `IsProjective` lands in Mathlib), `L` a line bundle on `X`
308	(relatively very ample), `E` a coherent `O_X`-module, and
309	`Φ ∈ ℚ[λ]`. Then the Quot functor `Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S}` of `QuotFunctor`
310	is representable by an `S`-scheme.
311	
312	We package the conclusion as the existence of `Q : Over S` together with
313	a `Functor.RepresentableBy Q` witness for `QuotFunctor π L E Φ`; the
314	*projectivity* of `Q ⟶ S` (and the universal quotient
315	`q^univ : π^*_Q E ↠ F^univ` on `X ×_S Q^{Φ,L}`) is implicit in the
316	construction (Plücker-embedded into a projective Grassmannian over `S`)
317	and is iter-177+ refinement work.
318	
319	iter-177+: the body follows the four-step Nitsure §5 proof
320	(boundedness ⟶ Grassmannian embedding ⟶ flattening stratification ⟶
321	valuative-criterion closed embedding); the sub-lemmas live in
322	`lem:quot_boundedness`, `lem:quot_alpha_injective`,
323	`lem:quot_valuative_criterion`, and the existential reduction in
324	`lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W`. For the iter-176 file-skeleton the
325	body is a typed `sorry`. -/
326	theorem QuotScheme {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
327	    (π : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType π] [IsProper π]
328	    (L E : X.Modules) (Φ : Polynomial ℚ) :
329	    ∃ (Q : Over S), Nonempty ((QuotFunctor π L E Φ).RepresentableBy Q) := by
330	  sorry
331	
332	end Scheme

334	/-! ## §5. Cohomology and base change
335	
336	The Quot construction uses cohomology-and-base-change in two places: the
337	boundedness step (Nitsure §5 "Use of m-Regularity") and the Grassmannian
338	embedding (Nitsure §5 "Embedding Quot into Grassmannian"). We record the
339	relevant statement as a named theorem so the Lean encoding can cite it
340	directly.
341	
342	The Stacks 02KH form is the statement for higher direct images
343	`R^i f_*` on quasi-coherent sheaves; for the iter-176 file-skeleton we
344	state the `i = 0` form on `Scheme.Modules`, which is the substantive
345	content of `lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology(ii)` of Stacks 02KH. The
346	`R^i` form is iter-177+ work after higher-direct-image infrastructure
347	is in scope.
348	
349	Blueprint reference: `thm:flat_base_change_cohomology` (Stacks 02KH). -/
350	
351	/-! ### Flat base change of cohomology (Stacks tag 02KH, `i = 0` form)
352	
353	Let
354	```
355	   g'
356	X' ───→ X
357	│       │
358	f'      f
359	↓       ↓
360	S' ───→ S
361	   g
362	```
363	be a cartesian square of schemes with `g` flat and `f` quasi-compact
364	quasi-separated. Let `F` be a sheaf of `O_X`-modules. Then the canonical
365	base-change map `g* (f_* F) ⟶ f'_* ((g')* F)` is an isomorphism in
366	`S'.Modules`.
367	
368	(The full Stacks 02KH statement covers all higher direct images
369	`R^i f_* F` for `i ≥ 0`; the `i = 0` form encoded here is the
370	substantive content of `lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology(ii)` of
371	Stacks 02KH, with the `i ≥ 1` form post-iter-177 work after the
372	higher-direct-image bifunctor lands.)
373	
374	iter-177 (Lane QS-FLAT): the body constructs the canonical base-change
375	natural transformation via the mate equivalence of the
376	`pullback ⊣ pushforward` adjunction (Mathlib's `mateEquiv` of
377	`Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction`), then exhibits the iso
378	via the `canonicalBaseChangeMap_isIso` helper. The deep mathematical
379	content (Stacks tag 02KH / 02KE / 00H8) lives entirely in the helper;
380	it reduces affine-locally to: for a flat ring map `A → B` and an
381	`A`-algebra `R`, the canonical map `B ⊗_A H^i(X, F) → H^i(X_B, F_B)`
382	is an iso for any quasi-coherent `F` (the `i = 0` form is what we use).
383	The helper remains a typed `sorry` pending the affine-local reduction
384	+ algebraic flat base change; this is iter-178+ body work after
385	quasi-compact open-cover Mayer-Vietoris infrastructure is in scope. -/
386	
387	/-- The canonical base-change natural transformation `g* (f_* -) ⟶ f'_* ((g')* -)`
388	associated to a cartesian square
389	```
390	     g'
391	X' ─────→ X
392	│         │
393	f'        f
394	↓         ↓
395	S' ─────→ S
396	     g
397	```
398	in `Scheme`. Constructed as the *mate* (Beck–Chevalley transform)
399	under the `pullback ⊣ pushforward` adjunctions on sheaves of modules
400	of the canonical 2-isomorphism
401	`pullback g ⋙ pullback f' ≅ pullback f ⋙ pullback g'` coming from
402	`g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g`.
403	
404	This natural transformation always exists (no flatness needed). The
405	content of the flat base-change theorem (Stacks tag 02KH) is the
406	*isomorphism* claim under the hypotheses
407	`[QuasiCompact f] [QuasiSeparated f] [Flat g]`; that claim is the
408	helper `canonicalBaseChangeMap_isIso`. -/
409	noncomputable def canonicalBaseChangeMap
410	    {X X' S S' : Scheme.{u}}
411	    {f : X ⟶ S} {g : S' ⟶ S} {g' : X' ⟶ X} {f' : X' ⟶ S'}
412	    (sq : IsPullback g' f' f g) :
413	    Scheme.Modules.pushforward f ⋙ Scheme.Modules.pullback g ⟶
414	      Scheme.Modules.pullback g' ⋙ Scheme.Modules.pushforward f' :=
415	  CategoryTheory.mateEquiv
416	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f)
417	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f')
418	      (((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f' g) ≪≫

742	HELPER, iter-189 unbundling refactor).
743	
744	The substantive transport-and-intertwining helper: given a morphism `g : Y ⟶ X`
745	of schemes, a quasi-coherent module `N` on `X`, and affine opens
746	`V ⊆ X`, `U ⊆ Y` with `U ⊆ g⁻¹ V`, produces:
747	- a `Γ(Y, U)`-linear equiv between `TensorProduct Γ(X, V) Γ(Y, U) Γ(N, V)`
748	  and `Γ((pullback g).obj N, U)`, and
749	- a proof that this equiv sends `1 ⊗ x` to `pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap g N e x`
750	  (the Beck-Chevalley compatibility).
751	
752	The construction follows the iter-187 analogist-licensed Tilde route
753	(`analogies/quotscheme-isbasechange-tilde.md`):
754	  Step 1: identify `N|_V ≅ tilde Γ(N, V)` on `Spec Γ(X, V)` using
755	    `[N.IsQuasicoherent]` (extract a presentation on the affine open
756	    after transporting via `hV.isoSpec`).
757	  Step 2: pull back via `Spec.map φ : Spec Γ(Y, U) ⟶ Spec Γ(X, V)`,
758	    where `φ = g.appLE V U e`; apply `pullback_tildeIso` to obtain
759	    `(pullback (Spec.map φ)).obj (tilde Γ(N, V)) ≅
760	      tilde (Γ(Y, U) ⊗ Γ(N, V))` on `Spec Γ(Y, U)`.

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}

5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.b sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
8	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) -- a projective \(S\)-scheme representing the
9	% functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) with
10	% Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\) on every fiber. The Quot construction is the
11	% \emph{existence engine} behind the relative Picard functor: A.2.c
12	% (\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) wires this chapter together with
13	% the relative Picard functor (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}, A.1.c)
14	% through the Abel map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\), which factors
15	% through the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) -- the
16	% case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor. This chapter is gated on
17	% A.2.a (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}, written this iter) and
18	% A.1.a (\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}, on disk).
19	%
20	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The construction embeds Quot as a
21	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
22	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
23	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
24	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
25	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
26	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
27	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability; it is a
28	% candidate for promotion to its own chapter
29	% \texttt{Picard\_GrassmannianScheme.tex} (writer iter-175+) -- see "Notes

32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:quot_setup}
34	
35	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
36	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
37	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
38	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
39	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
40	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
41	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
42	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
43	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness; see~\)
44	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
45	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
46	
47	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1 and
48	% \S 5; FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
49	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500 +
50	% L2154-L2542). The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
51	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
52	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
53	% the verbatim source-quote in \ is taken from
54	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
55	% reference).
56	
57	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
58	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
59	
60	\begin{definition}\leanok
61	[Hilbert polynomial]
62	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
63	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
64	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
65	  % Polynomials" (read from
66	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
67	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
68	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
69	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
70	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
71	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
72	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
73	  %  the function
74	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
75	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
76	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
77	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
78	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
79	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
80	  %
81	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
82	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
83	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
84	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
85	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
86	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
87	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
88	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
89	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
90	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
91	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
92	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
93	  III.5.2.}
94	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
95	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
96	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), the
97	  \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\) relative to \(L\)} is the
98	  polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) characterised by
99	  \[
100	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
101	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
102	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
103	  \]
104	  (a polynomial in \(m\) by Snapper's Lemma; the sum is finite by coherence and
105	  properness). When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
106	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
107	  well-defined on connected components.
108	\end{definition}

110	\section{The Quot functor}
111	\label{sec:quot_functor}
112	
113	\begin{definition}\leanok
114	[Quot functor]
115	  \label{def:quot_functor}
116	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
117	  
118	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
119	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
120	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
121	  % + L481-L498).
122	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
123	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
124	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
125	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
126	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
127	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
128	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
129	  %
130	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
131	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
132	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
133	  %
134	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
135	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
136	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
137	  %
138	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
139	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
140	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
141	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
142	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
143	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
144	  %  well-defined, which gives
145	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
146	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
147	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
148	  %  [...]
149	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
150	  %  as a co-product
151	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
152	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
153	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
154	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
155	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
156	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
157	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
158	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
159	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
160	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
161	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
162	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
163	  \[
164	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
165	  \]
166	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
167	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
168	  \begin{enumerate}
169	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
170	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) and which is flat over \(T\);
171	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
172	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
173	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
174	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
175	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
176	      \(\Phi\).
177	  \end{enumerate}
178	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
179	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
180	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
181	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
182	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
183	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
184	\end{definition}
