2	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
3	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
4	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
5	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
6	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),
7	% 02ME (order at a point on a regular 1-dimensional scheme),
8	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
9	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
10	\section{Setup and motivation}
11	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
12	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
13	\[
14	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
15	  \,\colon\,
16	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
17	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
18	\]
19	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
20	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
21	Riemann--Roch dimension formula \(\ell(D) = \deg D + 1\) for \(\deg D \geq 0\) on
22	a genus-\(0\) curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
23	\(\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via \(1\) and a non-constant function
24	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)''
25	classification recovered as a degree-\(1\) morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) via
26	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
27	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
28	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
29	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
30	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
31	codimension-\(1\) cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
32	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
33	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
34	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
35	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
36	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
37	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
38	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
39	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
40	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
41	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
42	\(\mathrm{Div}(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \mathrm{codim} 1}} \mathbb Z\) on a
43	Noetherian integral scheme \(X\) satisfying Hartshorne's condition \((*)\), the
44	principal-divisor homomorphism \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on a
45	curve, the degree map \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) on a smooth proper
46	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
47	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
48	(\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
49	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
50	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on \(C\) defines a
51	finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
52	O_C(D)\( and the associated cohomology computation \)H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
53	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
54	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-\(0\) classification proper lives in
55	\texttt{RR.4}.
56	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
57	\((*)\):
58	% SOURCE QUOTE: "\((\ast)\) \(X\) is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
59	% is regular in codimension one."
60	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional \((*)\).}
61	\[
62	  (\ast)\qquad
63	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
64	  codimension one.}
65	\]
66	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
67	additionally assume \(X\) is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
68	an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), where Hartshorne's condition \((*)\) is
69	automatic.
70	\paragraph{Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).}
71	Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) does not bundle Hartshorne's four-clause
72	\((\ast)\) as a single typeclass. The chapter pins \((*)\) to a \emph{layered}
73	typeclass set that the prover-agent threads through the Lean signatures in
74	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean}:
75	\begin{itemize}
76	  \item \textbf{Basic formal-sum layer} (carrier of \ref{def:prime_divisor} and
77	    \ref{def:codim1_cycles}, the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\), the degree
78	    \ref{def:divisor_degree}, the degree-homomorphism
79	    \ref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}, and \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}): the
80	    only Mathlib typeclass invoked is \texttt{[IsIntegral X]}
81	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} via
82	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Properties}). Integrality of \(X\) already
83	    delivers \texttt{IrreducibleSpace X.carrier} (the carrier-level instance
84	    chain), so \(X\) has a unique generic point and the function field
85	    \texttt{X.functionField} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField})
86	    is a field; separatedness and noetherianness are \emph{not} required for
87	    the bare formal-sum data type.
88	  \item \textbf{Order/principal layer} (\ref{def:order_at_point},
89	    \ref{def:principal_divisor}, \ref{thm:principal_hom}): the Lean
90	    signature additionally threads
91	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}
92	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian}) and Hartshorne's
93	    regular-in-codimension-one clause. The latter has \emph{no} Mathlib
94	    typeclass shipping it directly; the chapter's pin is the project-side
95	    predicate ``for every \(x \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1\) the
96	    stalk \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) is a discrete valuation ring'', encodable as
97	    the conjunction
98	    $\forall x,\; \texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1 \Rightarrow
99	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing}\,(\mathcal O_{X,x})$. Iter-173+ may
100	    introduce a \texttt{Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne} predicate to
101	    abbreviate this; until then the order/principal lemmas thread the DVR
102	    hypothesis explicitly at each codim-1 point.
103	  \item \textbf{Curve layer} (\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}): the standing
104	    hypothesis tightens to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
105	    over \(\bar k\) with explicit integrality witness, namely
106	    \begin{quote}
107	      \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
108	       (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
109	       \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
110	       \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
111	    \end{quote}
112	    matching the standing typeclass set used by the rest of the project's
113	    curve API (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus},
114	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity}). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is
115	    automatic for this layer (\texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian]} from finite type
116	    over a field; regular-in-codim-1 from smoothness; separatedness from
117	    properness; integrality threaded).
118	\end{itemize}
119	\noindent
120	The \texttt{[IsIntegral X]} hypothesis is the only one threaded through the
121	\emph{statement} of every Lean signature in
122	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean} that mentions a
123	function field, principal divisor, or order. The noetherian-and-DVR
124	content lives inside the proof bodies (sub-build), where the prover threads
125	the appropriate stronger hypothesis at the use site.
126	\section{Codim-\(1\) cycle group}
127	\begin{definition}
128	\leanok
129	[Prime divisor of a scheme]
130	  \label{def:prime_divisor}
131	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor}
132	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" under
133	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
134	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (divisors).
135	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). A \emph{prime
136	  % divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral subscheme \(Y\) of codimension one."
137	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of prime divisor).}
138	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). A \emph{prime divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral
139	  subscheme \(Y \subset X\) of codimension one. Equivalently, by the standard
140	  generic-point correspondence (Hartshorne II \S6, p.~130, ``let $\eta \in
141	  Y\( be its generic point\ldots''), \)Y$ is determined by its generic point
142	  \(\eta \in X\): closed integral subschemes of codimension one of \(X\) are in
143	  natural bijection with points \(x \in X\) such that the closure
144	  \(\overline{\{x\}} \subset X\) has codimension one. The closure
145	  \(\overline{\{x\}}\), equipped with the \emph{reduced induced subscheme
146	  structure}, is automatically integral (irreducible, because the closure
147	  of a single point is irreducible, and reduced by construction); the only
148	  non-automatic condition is the codimension.
149	  \paragraph{Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).} The Lean structure
150	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor} bundles a point
151	  \texttt{point : X} together with a codimension-one witness, encoded via
152	  the Mathlib predicate
153	  \[
154	    \texttt{Order.coheight\ point\ =\ 1}
155	  \]
156	  on the specialisation preorder of the topological space underlying \(X\).
157	  Concretely, Mathlib's preorder on \texttt{X.carrier} is defined by
158	  ``\(x \leq y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x\)'' (i.e.\ \(x \in \overline{\{y\}}\);
159	  see \texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Specialization} and
160	  \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}). Under this convention the generic
161	  point of an integral scheme is the unique \emph{maximal} element of the
162	  preorder, so for the generic point \(\eta_Y\) of a prime divisor \(Y\) the
163	  only point strictly above \(\eta_Y\) is the generic point \(\eta_X\) of \(X\),
164	  and the chain \(\eta_Y < \eta_X\) has length one, giving
165	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ \(\eta_Y\)\ =\ 1}. The DVR/integrality content of
166	  ``the closure of \texttt{point} with the reduced subscheme structure is
167	  integral'' is automatic from \(\overline{\{\,\texttt{point}\,\}}\) being
168	  irreducible, so the Lean structure does not need a separate integrality
169	  field. The iter-172 placeholder \texttt{isCodim1AndIntegral : True} in
170	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:84--90} is to be
171	  refined to \texttt{coheight : Order.coheight point = 1} (named field, type
172	  \texttt{Prop}) in the iter-173 prover step, retaining the same
173	  \texttt{point} field for downstream defeq.
174	  \paragraph{Reference predicates considered and rejected.}
175	  Two alternatives were considered and rejected at the iter-172
176	  lean-vs-blueprint-checker stage:
177	  \begin{itemize}
178	    \item \texttt{IsIntegral\ (X.closedSubschemeOfPoint\ x)}: rejected
179	      because Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships \emph{no}
180	      \texttt{closedSubschemeOfPoint} constructor. The closest available
181	      object is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData}
182	      (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Basic}), which packages
183	      a quasi-coherent ideal sheaf but does not produce a reduced induced
184	      subscheme on the closure of a point. Building that constructor is
185	      itself a Mathlib-upstream-scale project (Stacks tag 01J9 ``reduced
186	      induced scheme structure''), outside the scope of \texttt{RR.1}.
187	    \item A bundled \texttt{Scheme.IdealSheafData}-based prime-divisor
188	      carrier: rejected because the chapter only needs the generic-point
189	      witness for downstream order/principal-divisor consumption; carrying
190	      an \texttt{IdealSheafData} adds redundant data the chapter never
191	      uses.
192	  \end{itemize}
193	  The \texttt{Order.coheight}-based encoding is therefore the
194	  minimum-Mathlib-dependence pin compatible with the chapter's downstream
195	  needs.
196	\end{definition}
197	\begin{definition}
198	\leanok
199	[Codim-\(1\) cycle group / Weil divisor group of a scheme]
200	  \label{def:codim1_cycles}
201	  
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor}
203	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
204	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "Weil divisor" under
205	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
206	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW.
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A \emph{Weil divisor} is an element of the free abelian
208	  % group \(\operatorname{Div} X\) generated by the prime divisors. We write a
209	  % divisor as \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\), where the \(Y_i\) are prime divisors, the
210	  % \(n_i\) are integers, and only finitely many \(n_i\) are different from
211	  % zero. If all the \(n_i \geq 0\), we say that \(D\) is \emph{effective}."
212	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (Weil divisor group).}
213	  Let \(X\) be a Noetherian integral separated scheme that is regular in
214	  codimension one (i.e.\ \(X\) satisfies \((*)\)). The \emph{Weil divisor group}
215	  \[
216	    \mathrm{Div}(X) \;=\; \bigoplus_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
217	    \mathbb Z \cdot Y
218	  \]
219	  is the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of \(X\)
220	  (\ref{def:codim1_cycles}); an element \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\) with finitely
221	  many nonzero coefficients \(n_i \in \mathbb Z\) is called a \emph{Weil
222	  divisor}, and is \emph{effective} when all \(n_i \geq 0\).
223	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
224	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor X} is given for an arbitrary
225	  scheme \texttt{X : Scheme} as the formal-sum data type
226	  \texttt{X.PrimeDivisor \(\to_0\) \(\mathbb Z\)}; integrality of \(X\) is
227	  \emph{not} required to form the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\) (it becomes
228	  necessary only at the order/principal-divisor layer where the function
229	  field appears). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is the standing convention in the
230	  prose but is not threaded into the typeclass arguments of this base
231	  declaration. See ``Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding'' above
232	  for the per-layer typeclass discipline.
233	\end{definition}
234	\section{Order of a rational function at a prime divisor}
235	\begin{definition}
236	\leanok
237	[Order of a rational function at a prime divisor]
238	  \label{def:order_at_point}
239	  
240	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order}
242	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) of a prime
243	  % divisor; "zero/pole of \(f\) along \(Y\)") (read from
244	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
245	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
246	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its
247	  % generic point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at
248	  % \(\eta\) is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the
249	  % function field of \(X\). We call the corresponding discrete valuation
250	  % \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
251	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function
252	  % on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has
253	  % a \emph{zero} along \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\)
254	  % has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
255	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\)).}
256	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a prime divisor \(Y \subset X\) with generic
257	  point \(\eta \in Y\), the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
258	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
259	  function field \(K(X)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\)
260	  the \emph{order of \(f\) along \(Y\)} is the integer
261	  \[
262	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
263	  \]
264	  where \(v_Y\) is the normalised discrete valuation associated to
265	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\). If \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{zero of
266	  order \(\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}; if \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{pole
267	  of order \(-\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}.
268	  Specialisation to a curve. If \(C\) is a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)
269	  and \(P \in C\) is a closed point, then \(P\) is automatically a prime
270	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the \(1\)-dimensional
271	  scheme \(C\)); the local ring \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation
272	  ring by smoothness, and \(\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)\) is the standard DVR
273	  valuation of \(f\) at \(P\).
274	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-175 pin, per analogist
275	  \texttt{dvr-rationalmap-order}).} The iter-175 Lean signature is
276	  \begin{quote}
277	    \texttt{noncomputable def order \{X : Scheme.\{u\}\} [IsIntegral X]\\
278	    \phantom{}\quad[IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)\\
279	    \phantom{}\quad[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]\\
280	    \phantom{}\quad(f : X.functionField) : \(\mathbb Z\)}
281	  \end{quote}
282	  with \emph{no} \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis (junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention,
283	  see below). The body is
284	  \[
285	    \texttt{order}\ Y\ f \;:=\;
286	      \texttt{WithZero.log}\ \bigl(
287	        \texttt{Ring.ordFrac}\ (X.\texttt{presheaf.stalk}\ Y.\texttt{point})\ f
288	      \bigr).
289	  \]
290	  \emph{Mathlib API path.}
291	  \begin{itemize}
292	    \item \texttt{Ring.ordFrac R : K \(\to\!\!*_0\) \(\mathbb Z^{m_0}\)}
293	      (with \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} = \texttt{WithZero (Multiplicative}\ \mathbb Z)\)),
294	      from \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic} (Stacks
295	      tag~\texttt{02MD}), is Mathlib's canonical monoid-with-zero hom
296	      extending the length-of-quotient valuation
297	      \texttt{Ring.ord} from a Noetherian Krull-dim-\(\leq 1\) ring to
298	      its fraction field via \texttt{Submonoid.LocalizationMap.lift\textsubscript{0}}.
299	      For a DVR-uniformised local ring its value on \(r \in R\) is the
300	      length of \(R/(r)\) (the DVR valuation); on \(a/b \in K\), it is
301	      \(\texttt{ord}(a) - \texttt{ord}(b)\). The hypotheses
302	      \texttt{[CommRing R] [Nontrivial R] [IsNoetherianRing R]\\
303	      [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]} are exactly the typeclasses the body
304	      synthesises from \([\texttt{IsIntegral}\ X]\) (delivers
305	      \texttt{IsDomain} hence \texttt{Nontrivial} on the stalk via
306	      \texttt{instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral},
307	      and \texttt{IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
308	      X.functionField} via
309	      \texttt{instIsFractionRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafFunctionField}),
310	      from \([\texttt{IsLocallyNoetherian}\ X]\) (delivers
311	      \texttt{IsNoetherianRing} on the stalk via
312	      \texttt{instIsNoetherianRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsLocallyNoetherian}),
313	      and from the explicitly threaded
314	      \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]}.
315	    \item \texttt{WithZero.log : \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} \to \mathbb Z\)}
316	      from \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero} is Mathlib's
317	      canonical projection to the additive value group with
318	      junk-on-zero: \texttt{log\_zero}: \(\log 0 = 0\);
319	      \texttt{log\_mul}: \(\log(x \cdot y) = \log x + \log y\) for
320	      \(x, y \neq 0\). This makes the downstream additive identities
321	      \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\), \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\),
322	      \(v_Y(1) = 0\) available as named lemmas for
323	      \ref{def:order_at_point}.
324	  \end{itemize}
325	  \emph{Junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention.} Setting \(f = 0\) in
326	  \texttt{X.functionField} feeds \(0\) into
327	  \texttt{Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)} (zero of the
328	  monoid-with-zero hom; \texttt{Ring.ordFrac} maps \(0\) to
329	  \(0 \in \mathbb Z^{m_0}\)); then \texttt{WithZero.log 0 = 0} so
330	  \(\texttt{order}\ Y\ 0 = 0\). This is the canonical Mathlib junk
331	  choice; no separate \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis is threaded on the
332	  signature.
333	  \emph{In-tree gap-fill (project-side; Mathlib upstream opportunity).}
334	  Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) has no direct bridge from the
335	  topological condition \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ Y.\texttt{point} = 1\)
336	  on \texttt{X.carrier} to the algebraic condition
337	  \(\texttt{Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)}\) on the
338	  stalk. The missing fact (Stacks tag~\texttt{02IZ} / \texttt{005X}) is
339	  the topological identity ``coheight of \(x\) in the scheme's
340	  specialisation preorder equals the height of the prime ideal
341	  corresponding to \(x\) in any affine chart''; the algebraic step
342	  ``ideal height \(=\) stalk Krull dim'' is in Mathlib as
343	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
344	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height}) and
345	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
346	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}). The iter-175
347	  workaround is to thread the explicit instance
348	  \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} on
349	  \texttt{order} (and downstream consumers); a future two-line
350	  Mathlib upstream PR pinning the coheight-height bridge would let
351	  the project drop the explicit argument in favour of synthesis from
352	  \texttt{coheight Y.point = 1} via the \texttt{PrimeDivisor} carrier.
353	\end{definition}
354	\section{Divisor of a closed point on a curve}
355	\begin{definition}
356	\leanok
357	[Divisor of a closed point on a curve]
358	  \label{def:divisor_closed_point}
359	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
360	  
361	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint}
362	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
363	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154):
364	  % "A prime divisor is just a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can
365	  % be written \(D = \sum n_i P_i\), where the \(P_i\) are closed points, and
366	  % \(n_i \in \mathbf Z\)."
367	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now we come to the study of divisors on curves. If \(X\)
368	  % is a nonsingular curve, then \(X\) satisfies the condition \((\ast)\) used
369	  % above, so we can talk about divisors on \(X\). A prime divisor is just
370	  % a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can be written $D = \sum n_i
371	  % P_i\(, where the \)P_i\( are closed points, and \)n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
372	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (``Divisors on Curves'').}
373	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over a field \(k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
374	  closed point. The \emph{Weil divisor associated to \(P\)} is the element
375	  \[
376	    [P] \;:=\; 1 \cdot P \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C)
377	  \]
378	  i.e.\ the prime divisor \(P\) with multiplicity one. The assignment is
379	  well-defined because the closed point \(P\) is automatically a codimension-\(1\)
380	  integral closed subscheme of the \(1\)-dimensional integral scheme \(C\).
381	  Conversely, every prime divisor on \(C\) is of this form, so an arbitrary
382	  divisor on \(C\) is a finite formal \(\mathbb Z\)-linear combination
383	  \(\sum n_i [P_i]\) of closed points.
384	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-174 pin).} The Lean signature
385	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint} accepts a
386	  scheme \texttt{C : Scheme.\{u\}}, a point \texttt{P : C}, and a
387	  hypothesis \texttt{(\_hP : IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C))}. It does
388	  \emph{not} typeclass-pin ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' at the
389	  carrier level, and it does \emph{not} thread the
390	  one-dimensional-integral-scheme content into the declaration head.
391	  Instead the iter-174 encoding is \emph{junk-defined} outside the
392	  codim-\(1\) regime:
393	  \[
394	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP\ :=\
395	      \begin{cases}
396	        \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1 &
397	          \text{if } h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1,\\
398	        0 & \text{otherwise.}
399	      \end{cases}
400	  \]
401	  On the off-branch --- where \texttt{P} is not a codimension-one point
402	  (e.g.\ \texttt{P} is the generic point of \texttt{C}, or a closed point
403	  of codimension \(\geq 2\) on a higher-dimensional scheme) ---
404	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} returns the zero divisor \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). This
405	  convention lets the Lean signature avoid threading
406	  \texttt{[OneDimensional C]} / \texttt{[IsIntegral C]} typeclass payload
407	  at the declaration head; the well-definedness consumer must instead
408	  invoke the bridge equation lemmas
409	  \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single} and \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
410	  to expose the two branches at the point of use.
411	  On the intended regime (one-dimensional integral scheme \(C\), \(P\) a
412	  closed point), the chapter's mathematical statement
413	  \([P] := 1 \cdot P \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) is recovered by combining
414	  \ref{def:divisor_closed_point} with the chain
415	  \(\texttt{IsClosed \{P\}} \Rightarrow \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1\)
416	  that the prover threads inside the consumer (using the
417	  one-dimensional-integrality content).
418	\end{definition}
419	\begin{lemma}

433	  \]

436	  \leanok
437	  Unfolds the dependent-\texttt{if} in the definition of

439	  positive branch using the hypothesis \(h\).
440	\end{proof}

464	\section{Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base}
465	\begin{definition}
466	\leanok
467	[Degree of a divisor on a curve over \(\bar k\)]
468	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
469	  \label{def:divisor_degree}
470	  
471	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree}
472	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
473	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
474	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE0.
475	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We define the \emph{degree} of \(D\) to be \(\sum n_i\)."
476	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (definition of degree on a
477	  nonsingular curve).}
478	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
479	  \(\bar k\). The \emph{degree} of a Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
480	  \mathrm{Div}(C)$ is the integer
481	  \[
482	    \deg(D) \;:=\; \sum_i n_i \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
483	  \]
484	  The definition uses that every closed point of \(C\) has residue field
485	  \(\bar k\) (so each prime divisor contributes degree one); over a general
486	  field \(k\) the same definition with the residue-field-degree weight
487	  \(\deg(D) := \sum_i n_i \, [\kappa(P_i) : k]\) recovers the
488	  \emph{geometric} degree, but the project's RR bridge needs only the
489	  \(\bar k\) specialisation.
490	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
491	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree} is given for an
492	  arbitrary scheme \texttt{X : Scheme.\{u\}} as the literal sum of
493	  coefficients via \texttt{Finsupp.sum D (fun \_ n => n)}; the typeclass
494	  set ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' is \emph{not} pinned on the
495	  base declaration. Mathematically this assigns to every Weil divisor
496	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on any scheme \(X\) the integer-valued sum of its
497	  multiplicities; for a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) this coincides
498	  with the curve-theoretic degree of the prose (every closed point has
499	  residue field \(\bar k\), so the residue-field-degree weight is identically
500	  one). The chapter's prose pins the curve hypothesis for clarity and to
501	  flag that, on a scheme with non-trivial residue-field extensions, the
502	  bare-sum signature does \emph{not} compute the geometric degree; the
503	  curve hypothesis is threaded at the call sites that consume
504	  \texttt{degree} for arithmetic statements
505	  (\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}, \texttt{RR.2}--\texttt{RR.4}).
506	\end{definition}
507	\begin{theorem}
508	\leanok
509	[The degree map is a group homomorphism]
510	  \label{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
511	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom}
512	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
513	  \(\bar k\). The degree map of \ref{def:divisor_degree} is a group
514	  homomorphism
515	  \[
516	    \deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \longrightarrow \mathbb Z,
517	    \qquad
518	    \deg(D_1 + D_2) \;=\; \deg(D_1) + \deg(D_2),
519	    \qquad
520	    \deg(-D) \;=\; -\deg(D).
521	  \]
522	\end{theorem}
523	\begin{proof}
524	  \leanok
525	  Immediate from the definition: \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) is the free abelian group on the
526	  set of closed points of \(C\), and \(\deg\) is the unique group homomorphism
527	  sending each generator \([P]\) to \(1 \in \mathbb Z\) (equivalently: the sum
528	  of the multiplicities). Both additivity and negation thus reduce to the
529	  corresponding identities for finite sums of integers.
530	\end{proof}
531	\section{Principal divisors}
532	\begin{definition}
533	\leanok
534	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point}
535	[Principal divisor of a rational function]
536	  \label{def:principal_divisor}
537	  
538	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}
539	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1 + the definition of \(\operatorname{div}(f) = (f)\),
540	  % p.~131 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
541	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\), and let \(f \in K^\ast\)
542	  % be a nonzero function on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f) = 0\) for all except finitely
543	  % many prime divisors \(Y\)."
544	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\) and let \(f \in K^\ast\).
545	  % We define the \emph{divisor of \(f\)}, denoted \((f)\), by
546	  % \((f) = \sum v_Y(f) \cdot Y\), where the sum is taken over all prime
547	  % divisors \(Y\) of \(X\). By the lemma, this is a finite sum, hence is a
548	  % divisor. Any divisor which is equal to the divisor of a function is
549	  % called a \emph{principal divisor}."
550	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1 and the following definition,
551	  p.~131.}
552	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\),
553	  Hartshorne's Lemma~6.1 asserts that \(\ord_Y(f) = 0\) for all but finitely
554	  many prime divisors \(Y\) on \(X\); hence the formal sum
555	  \[
556	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;:=\; \sum_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
557	    \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(X)
558	  \]
559	  has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor, called the
560	  \emph{principal divisor of \(f\)}. On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\)
561	  the same definition specialises to
562	  \(\mathrm{div}(f) = \sum_{P \in C \text{ closed}} \ord_P(f) \cdot [P]\), the formal
563	  sum over closed points.
564	\end{definition}
565	\begin{theorem}
566	\leanok
567	[The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism]
568	  \label{thm:principal_hom}
569	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom}
570	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, the paragraph after the principal-divisor
571	  % definition, p.~131 (read from
572	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
573	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Note that if \(f, g \in K^\ast\), then \((f/g) = (f) - (g)\)
574	  % because of the properties of valuations. Therefore sending a function
575	  % \(f\) to its divisor \((f)\) gives a homomorphism of the multiplicative
576	  % group \(K^\ast\) to the additive group \(\operatorname{Div} X\), and the
577	  % image, which consists of the principal divisors, is a subgroup of
578	  % \(\operatorname{Div} X\)."
579	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (group-homomorphism remark).}
580	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). The principal-divisor map
581	  \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) is a group homomorphism from the
582	  multiplicative group of nonzero rational functions to the additive group
583	  of Weil divisors. Concretely
584	  \[
585	    \mathrm{div}(fg) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f) + \mathrm{div}(g),
586	    \qquad
587	    \mathrm{div}(f^{-1}) \;=\; -\mathrm{div}(f),
588	    \qquad
589	    \mathrm{div}(1) \;=\; 0.
590	  \]
591	\end{theorem}
592	\begin{proof}
593	  \leanok
594	  Each prime divisor \(Y\) on \(X\) contributes a discrete valuation \(v_Y\) on
595	  \(K(X)\) (\ref{def:principal_divisor}); by the standard DVR axioms,
596	  \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\) and \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\) for $f, g \in
597	  K(X)^{\times}\(, and \)v_Y(1) = 0$. Summing these identities over all prime
598	  divisors of \(X\) (a sum that has finite support on each factor by
599	  \ref{thm:principal_hom}, so the support of the sum is the union
600	  of the two finite supports and remains finite) gives the three displayed
601	  identities.
602	\end{proof}
603	\begin{theorem}
604	\leanok
605	[Principal divisors have degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve]
606	  \label{thm:principal_deg_zero}
607	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero}
608	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138 (read from
609	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 155).
610	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE3.
611	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.10. \emph{A principal divisor on a complete
612	  % nonsingular curve \(X\) has degree zero. Consequently the degree function
613	  % induces a surjective homomorphism $\deg \colon \operatorname{Cl} X \to
614	  % \mathbf Z$.}"
615	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138.}
616	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
617	  \(\bar k\). For every nonzero rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) the
618	  associated principal divisor has degree zero:
619	  \[
620	    \deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) \;=\; 0 \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
621	  \]
622	\end{theorem}
623	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Let \(f \in K(X)^\ast\). If \(f \in k\), then
624	% \((f) = 0\), so there is nothing to prove. If \(f \notin k\), then the
625	% inclusion of fields \(k(f) \subset K(X)\) induces a finite morphism
626	% \(\varphi \colon X \to \mathbf P^1\). It is a morphism by (I, 6.12). We
627	% have \((f) = \varphi^\ast(\{0\} - \{\infty\})\). Since \(\{0\} - \{\infty\}\)
628	% is a divisor of degree 0 on \(\mathbf P^1\), we conclude that \((f)\) has
629	% degree 0 on \(X\)."
630	\begin{proof}
631	  Following Hartshorne's Corollary~6.10. If \(f \in \bar k \subset K(C)\) is
632	  a constant scalar, then \(f\) has no zeros or poles, so \(\mathrm{div}(f) = 0\) and
633	  \(\deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) = 0\). Otherwise the inclusion of fields
634	  \(\bar k(f) \subset K(C)\) exhibits \(K(C)\) as a finite extension of the
635	  rational function field \(\bar k(f) \cong \bar k(t)\), so the corresponding
636	  morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) is finite (by the
637	  smooth-proper-curve / function-field correspondence on which a curve is
638	  determined by its function field, classical I.6.12). The principal
639	  divisor \(\mathrm{div}(f)\) equals the pullback \(\varphi^{*}([0] - [\infty])\)
640	  along \(\varphi\) of the degree-\(0\) divisor \([0] - [\infty]\) on
641	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\); since pullback along a finite morphism
642	  multiplies degrees by \(\deg(\varphi)\), we conclude
643	  $\deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg([0] - [\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)
644	  \cdot 0 = 0$.
645	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The two auxiliary statements ``the inclusion
646	  \(\bar k(f) \subset K(C)\) defines a finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)''
647	  and ``pullback along a finite morphism of curves multiplies degrees by
648	  the degree of the morphism'' (Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9, the
649	  multiplicativity of degree) are themselves separable sub-lemmas of
650	  \texttt{RR.1}; they are stated here for completeness and slotted to be
651	  closed in a follow-up iteration. The signature
652	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal\_degree\_zero}
653	  is recorded as the public face of this theorem and is depended on by
654	  the \texttt{RR.3}/\texttt{RR.4} chain.
655	\end{proof}
656	\section{Positive part of a Weil divisor}
657	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase): added to host the Pin 2 corrective per
658	% `task_results/AlgebraicJacobian_RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.lean.md`
659	% (iter-189). `Hom.poleDivisor` is being refactored to return the
660	% positive part of `principal (φ^* t_∞)` so that
661	% `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is mathematically correct (the principal
662	% divisor has degree 0 by `thm:principal_deg_zero`; only its
663	% positive part can have positive degree).
664	\begin{definition}
665	
666	
667	\leanok
668	[Positive part of a Weil divisor]
669	  \label{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart}
670	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart}
671	  \textit{Source: Project-bespoke (Hartshorne II.6.10 phrasing).}
672	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying the codim-1 hypotheses of
673	  \ref{def:codim1_cycles}. For a Weil divisor
674	  \(D = \sum_Y n_Y \cdot Y \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\), the \emph{positive part}
675	  \((D)_0 \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) is the Weil divisor
676	  \[
677	    (D)_0 \;:=\; \sum_{Y \,:\, n_Y > 0} n_Y \cdot Y.
678	  \]
679	  Concretely, \((D)_0\) is obtained from \(D\) by replacing each coefficient
680	  \(n_Y\) with \(\max(n_Y, 0)\). Equivalently, in the \(\Finsupp{\mathrm{PrimeDivisor}(X)}{\mathbb Z}\)
681	  presentation, the positive part is obtained either by the pointwise lattice
682	  join with the zero divisor — \(\,(D)_0 = D \sqcup 0\,\) — or, equivalently
683	  and more transparently for typeclass synthesis, by mapping the coefficients
684	  through the zero-preserving function \(n \mapsto n \sqcup 0\):
685	  \(\,(D)_0 = \mathtt{Finsupp.mapRange}\;(n \mapsto n \sqcup 0)\;D\). The two
686	  forms agree by the universal property of \(\mathtt{Finsupp.mapRange}\). The
687	  Lean implementation uses the \(\mathtt{mapRange}\) form to avoid the
688	  noncomputable \(\Finsupp.\mathtt{semilatticeSup}\) synthesis. The complementary \emph{negative part}
689	  \((D)_\infty\) is defined by \((D)_\infty := (-D)_0\), so that
690	  \(D = (D)_0 - (D)_\infty\) is the canonical decomposition into an
691	  effective (non-negative-coefficient) divisor minus an effective divisor.
692	  On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\), where the prime divisors
693	  are the closed points and the coefficients are integers, the positive part
694	  reduces to keeping only the closed points whose multiplicity is positive
695	  — i.e. precisely the zeros of any rational function whose principal
696	  divisor is \(D\), in the case \(D = \mathrm{div}(f)\).
697	\end{definition}
698	\begin{lemma}
699	
700	\leanok
701	[Degree of the positive part of \(\mathrm{div}(f)\) for a non-constant rational
702	function equals the function-field extension degree]
703	  \label{lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}
704	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}
705	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Proposition~6.9, p.~137 (multiplicativity
706	  % of degree under finite pullback) (read from
707	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
708	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (pullback of divisors under finite
709	  % morphisms), tag 0BE6 (degree under finite morphism).
710	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.9. \emph{Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a finite
711	  % morphism of nonsingular curves. Then for any divisor \(D\) on \(Y\),
712	  % \(\deg(f^\ast D) = [K(X) : K(Y)] \cdot \deg D\).}"
713	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Proposition~6.9, p.~137.}
714	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over an
715	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let
716	  \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism.
717	  Let \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) be a local parameter at \(\infty \in \mathbb P^1\)
718	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
719	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)). Write \(f := \varphi^{\#} t_\infty
720	  \in K(C)\) for the pullback of \(t_\infty\) along \(\varphi\). Then \(f\) is
721	  nonzero (because \(\varphi\) is non-constant; equivalently \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
722	  is a proper closed subscheme of \(C\)) and the degree of the positive
723	  part of the principal divisor of \(f\) equals the function-field
724	  extension degree:
725	  \[
726	    \deg\bigl((\mathrm{div}(f))_0\bigr)
727	    \;=\; \bigl[\,K(C) \,:\, \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,\bigr]
728	    \;=\; \mathrm{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C).
729	  \]
730	  Equivalently, \((\mathrm{div}(f))_0 = \varphi^{*}[\infty]\) (the effective
731	  preimage divisor of \(\infty\)) and \(\deg(\varphi^{*}[\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)\).
732	\end{lemma}
733	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. The morphism \(f \colon X \to Y\) is finite,
734	% so locally on \(Y\) corresponds to a finite extension of Dedekind domains
735	% \(A \subset B\). For each closed point \(P \in Y\) the ramification--inertia
736	% formula gives \(\sum_{Q | P} e_Q f_Q = [K(X) : K(Y)]\), where the sum runs
737	% over closed points \(Q \in X\) above \(P\). Summing this identity over
738	% \(P \in Y\) using the affine cover and noting that \(D = \sum_P n_P [P]\)
739	% gives \(\deg(f^\ast D) = \sum_{P} n_P \sum_{Q | P} e_Q f_Q
740	% = \sum_P n_P [K(X) : K(Y)] = [K(X) : K(Y)] \deg(D)\)."
741	\begin{proof}
742	  Following Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9 specialised to the divisor
743	  \(D = [\infty]\) on \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\). A non-constant morphism
744	  between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is automatically finite (the
745	  image is a closed irreducible subset that is not a point and not empty,
746	  hence all of \(\mathbb P^1\); a surjective morphism between smooth proper
747	  irreducible curves of equal dimension is proper + quasi-finite, hence
748	  finite; cf.\ Hartshorne II.6.8). Hence the function-field extension
749	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) is finite of degree
750	  \(d := [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)] = \dim_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\).
751	  Pick an affine open \(\mathrm{Spec}(A) \subset \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) containing
752	  \(\infty\); explicitly take \(A = \bar k[t_\infty]\) the standard chart at
753	  \(\infty\), with \(\infty\) corresponding to the maximal ideal
754	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty = (t_\infty) \subset A\). The preimage
755	  \(\mathrm{Spec}(B) := \varphi^{-1}(\mathrm{Spec}(A))\) is an affine open of \(C\)
756	  with \(B\) a finite (and integrally closed, by \(C\) smooth) extension of
757	  \(A\); both are Dedekind domains. Apply
758	  \texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia} (Mathlib,
759	  \texttt{Mathlib.NumberTheory.RamificationInertia.Basic}) to the
760	  ring-theoretic extension \(A \to B\) at the maximal ideal
761	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty\):
762	  \[
763	    \sum_{Q \,\colon\, \mathfrak m_\infty \subset Q} e_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty} \cdot f_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}
764	    \;=\; \dim_{A / \mathfrak m_\infty}\!\bigl(B / \mathfrak m_\infty B\bigr)
765	    \;=\; \dim_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) \;=\; d,
766	  \]
767	  where the second equality uses
768	  \texttt{Ideal.finrank\_quotient\_map} (which identifies the residue-field
769	  quotient with the function-field extension degree for a Dedekind extension
770	  at a maximal ideal). The closed points \(Q \subset \mathrm{Spec}(B)\) above
771	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty\) are precisely the closed points
772	  \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty) \subset C\); the ramification index
773	  \(e_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}\) is the order \(\ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty)\)
774	  of the pulled-back local parameter at \(P\); the residue degree
775	  \(f_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}\) is \([\kappa(P) : \bar k] = 1\) (every closed
776	  point of \(C\) over \(\bar k\) has residue field \(\bar k\)). Therefore
777	  \[
778	    \sum_{P \,\in\, \varphi^{-1}(\infty)} \ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty) \;=\; d
779	    \;=\; \mathrm{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C).
780	  \]
781	  The left-hand side equals the degree of the positive part of
782	  \(\mathrm{div}(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty)\), because: at every closed point
783	  \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\) the order is \(\geq 1\) (pullback of a local
784	  parameter under a finite morphism is itself a uniformiser-power, hence
785	  has positive order at every preimage); at every closed point
786	  \(P \in C \setminus \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\) the rational function
787	  \(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty\) is regular and nonzero (because \(t_\infty\) is
788	  regular and nonzero on \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)), hence the
789	  order at such a \(P\) is \(\geq 0\); the points where the order is
790	  \emph{strictly} positive are exactly the points of \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty)\).
791	  Therefore
792	  \(\,(\mathrm{div}(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))_0 = \sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
793	  \ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty) \cdot [P] = \varphi^{*}[\infty]\,\), and
794	  taking degree completes the proof.
795	\end{proof}
796	\section{Divisor class group}
797	We follow Hartshorne II.6 (pp.~131--132) and define the divisor class group
798	of a curve as the cokernel of the principal-divisor homomorphism. This is
799	the \emph{Picard group} of \(C\) in the curve-specific sense; the line-bundle
800	interpretation \(\Cl(C) \cong \Pic(C)\) requires the construction of
801	\(\mathcal O_C(D)\), which lives in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3} and is
802	out of scope here.
803	\begin{definition}
804	\leanok
805	[Linear equivalence of divisors]
806	  \label{def:linear_equivalence}
807	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.LinearEquivalence}
808	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~131 (definition of linear equivalence
809	  % and of \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)) (read from
810	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
811	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). Two divisors \(D\)
812	  % and \(D'\) are said to be \emph{linearly equivalent}, written \(D \sim D'\),
813	  % if \(D - D'\) is a principal divisor. The group \(\operatorname{Div} X\) of
814	  % all divisors divided by the subgroup of principal divisors is called
815	  % the \emph{divisor class group} of \(X\), and is denoted by
816	  % \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)."
817	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (linear equivalence and
818	  \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)).}
819	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). Two Weil divisors \(D, D' \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) are
820	  \emph{linearly equivalent}, written \(D \sim D'\), if and only if there
821	  exists \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\) with
822	  \[
823	    D - D' \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f) \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(X).
824	  \]
825	  The relation \(\sim\) is an equivalence relation on \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\) (the
826	  reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity all transport from the analogous
827	  identities \(\mathrm{div}(1) = 0\), \(\mathrm{div}(f^{-1}) = -\mathrm{div}(f)\), $\mathrm{div}(fg) =
828	  \mathrm{div}(f) + \mathrm{div}(g)$ via \ref{thm:principal_hom}); the corresponding
829	  quotient group \(\Cl(X) := \mathrm{Div}(X) / \mathrm{im}(\div)\) is the
830	  \emph{divisor class group} of \(X\). On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over
831	  \(\bar k\), \ref{thm:principal_deg_zero} implies that the degree map
832	  \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) descends to a homomorphism
833	  \(\deg \colon \Cl(C) \to \mathbb Z\).
834	\end{definition}
835	\section{Out of scope}
836	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.1} and
837	are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
838	\begin{itemize}
839	  \item The line-bundle construction \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to a Weil
840	    divisor \(D\), the calculation of its global sections, and the
841	    identification \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when
842	    \(D \sim D'\), lives in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
843	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added).
844	  \item The Riemann--Roch dimension formula
845	    \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) and its genus-\(0\) specialisation
846	    \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) live in \texttt{RR.2}
847	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RRFormula.tex}, to be added).
848	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
849	    curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)''
850	    (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5) is the headline of \texttt{RR.4}
851	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added)
852	    and assembles \texttt{RR.1}/\texttt{RR.2}/\texttt{RR.3} into the
853	    \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) together
854	    with its degree-\(1\) identification.
855	  \item \emph{Cartier divisors} (locally principal divisors on a possibly
856	    singular scheme) are out of scope: the project's curves are smooth, so
857	    Weil and Cartier divisors coincide, and the simpler Weil form suffices.
858	    A future Mathlib upstream PR could introduce the Cartier formalism.
859	  \item \emph{Higher-codimension cycles} (the Chow group
860	    \(\bigoplus_i \mathrm{CH}^i(X)\)) are out of scope: this chapter is
861	    strictly curve-focused.
862	  \item The proof of \ref{thm:principal_deg_zero} factors through two
863	    auxiliary statements (``finite morphism induced by a non-constant
864	    rational function'' and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite
865	    pullback'', Hartshorne II.6.9) which are themselves separable
866	    sub-lemmas of \texttt{RR.1}; they are flagged in the proof body but
867	    deliberately not unfolded as standalone declarations here.
868	\end{itemize}

1	# Analogy: Pin 2 — `morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor` (Hartshorne II.6.9 / IV §2)
2	
3	## Mode
4	api-alignment
5	
6	## Slug
7	ratcurveiso-pins (Pin 2)
8	
9	## Iteration
10	181
11	
12	## Question
13	
14	Does Mathlib have either of (a) `Scheme.Hom.degree` for a morphism between
15	smooth proper curves over a field, (b) the pullback of a Weil divisor
16	`f^*D` on a scheme, (c) the Hartshorne II.6.9 identity
17	`deg(f^*D) = deg(f) · deg(D)`? If yes, the project should align with the
18	Mathlib idiom for
19	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor`
20	(`AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean:296-306`).
21	
22	## Project artifact(s)
23	
24	- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean:296-306` —
25	  `theorem morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor` with body `sorry`. The
26	  statement asserts `∃ (d : ℕ) (D : C.left.WeilDivisor), 0 < d ∧
27	  Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D = (d : ℤ)` for a non-constant
28	  `φ : C ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar k̄`.
29	- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:92-298` — the
30	  project-bespoke `Scheme.PrimeDivisor`, `Scheme.WeilDivisor`,
31	  `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree`, `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal` (all
32	  `sorry`-bodied skeletons).
33	
34	## Decisions identified
35	
36	Three coupled decisions:
37	
38	1. Does Mathlib already define the *degree of a morphism* between
39	   schemes (or specifically between integral / smooth-proper-curve
40	   schemes)?
41	2. Does Mathlib define the *Weil-divisor pullback* `f^* : Div(Y) → Div(X)`
42	   for a (suitable) morphism of schemes?
43	3. Does Mathlib offer the multiplicativity-of-degree formula
44	   `deg(f^*D) = deg(f) · deg(D)`?
45	
46	### Decision 1: degree of a morphism between schemes
47	
48	- **Mathlib idiom**: NONE. Search confirms Mathlib has no
49	  `Scheme.Hom.degree`, no `Morphism.degree`, no
50	  `AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField.transcendenceDegree`. The closest is:
51	  - `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField` —
52	    `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.functionField (X : Scheme) [IrreducibleSpace X] : CommRingCat`
53	    (the field at the generic point, abbreviation `K(X)`).
54	  - `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField:instIsFractionRing...` —
55	    when `X` is integral, `IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk x) X.functionField`.
56	  - `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Finite` — `IsFinite f` /
57	    `IsFinite.iff_isProper_and_isAffineHom`.
58	  - `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.RationalMap` — `Scheme.RationalMap`,
59	    `ofFunctionField` / `fromFunctionField`.
60	  - `Mathlib.FieldTheory.SeparableDegree.Field.finSepDegree`,
61	    `Mathlib.FieldTheory.IntermediateField.Adjoin.Basic.IntermediateField.adjoin.finrank`,
62	    `Module.finrank` over a field extension.
63	
64	  So the **field-theoretic ingredient** for "degree of a finite morphism
65	  of integral schemes" — namely `[K(C) : K(C')]` via
66	  `Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField` — is present, but the
67	  *bridge* (i.e. wrap "finrank of the function-field extension" as a
68	  named `Scheme.Hom.degree` with the multiplicativity API) is absent.
69	
70	- **Project's current path**: define the degree implicitly inside the
71	  existence statement `∃ d, 0 < d ∧ D.degree = (d : ℤ)`. The `d` here is
72	  conceptually `[K(C) : k̄(ℙ¹)]` but the lemma never names a separate
73	  `degree_of_morphism` definition.
74	
75	- **Gap**: divergent-and-isolated. Mathlib has neither a `degree` API
76	  for `Scheme.Hom` nor a Weil-divisor type for schemes — the project
77	  could not "align" even if it tried. The current statement (existence
78	  of a positive-degree divisor) is the right substantive content given
79	  the project-bespoke `Scheme.WeilDivisor` type.
80	
81	- **Cost of divergence**: zero, because no Mathlib API exists to
82	  fragment with. The eventual *body* of the lemma will route through
83	  `Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField` + the
84	  project's own `WeilDivisor.pullback` (to be built) + a coordinate-by-
85	  coordinate identification.
86	
87	- **Verdict**: **PROCEED**. Mathlib has no scheme-level morphism-degree
88	  API to align with; the project's statement is the correct shape.
89	
90	### Decision 2: Weil-divisor pullback `f^* : Div(Y) → Div(X)`
91	
92	- **Mathlib idiom**: NONE for schemes. The only "divisor pullback" in
93	  Mathlib is for the complex-analytic case:
94	  - `Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor.MeromorphicOn.divisor` (complex
95	    analytic; takes a meromorphic function on `U ⊆ 𝕜` and produces a
96	    divisor).
97	
98	  For affine commutative rings Mathlib has:
99	  - `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup.CommRing.Pic` (the Picard group as an
100	    isomorphism class of invertible modules) — but this is for a single
101	    commutative ring, not for a scheme, and it does not expose a
102	    "divisor of a function" or pullback API.
103	
104	  The most directly relevant Mathlib infrastructure for the eventual
105	  body is:
106	  - `Mathlib.NumberTheory.RamificationInertia.Basic.Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia`
107	    (for a finite extension of Dedekind domains, the sum
108	    `Σ_{P over p} e(P|p) · f(P|p) = [L:K]`). This is the algebraic
109	    incarnation of Hartshorne II.6.9 specialised to Dedekind bases.
110	  - `Mathlib.NumberTheory.RamificationInertia.Basic.Ideal.finrank_quotient_map`
111	    (the closely related `finrank` identity).
112	
113	- **Project's current path**: `Scheme.WeilDivisor.pullback` does NOT
114	  exist in `WeilDivisor.lean` yet; the iter-178+ body of Pin 2 is
115	  expected to introduce a project-private pullback construction (per
116	  the lemma's docstring comment at L283-292).
117	
118	- **Gap**: divergent-and-isolated. Same reason as Decision 1: Mathlib
119	  has no scheme-level WeilDivisor to pull back.
120	
121	- **Cost of divergence**: zero now; non-zero once the project starts
122	  building. The natural body uses `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` on
123	  each affine chart (pick an affine open `Spec A ⊂ C'` covering `∞`,
124	  then the preimage `Spec B ⊂ C` is finite over `Spec A` with
125	  `A → B` Dedekind-of-Dedekind, and `Σ_{Q over P} e(Q|P) · f(Q|P) = d`).
126	  This is `~50-100 LOC` glue around the existing Mathlib lemma.
127	
128	- **Verdict**: **PROCEED** (with `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` as
129	  the body-engine for iter-178+).
130	
131	### Decision 3: multiplicativity of degree
132	
133	- **Mathlib idiom**: `Mathlib.NumberTheory.RamificationInertia.Basic`
134	  (`sum_ramification_inertia`, `ramificationIdx_mul_inertiaDeg_of_isLocalRing`)
135	  is the algebraic statement. There is no scheme-level wrapper.
136	- **Verdict**: **PROCEED** (consume the ring-level statement inside
137	  the body).
138	
139	## Recommendation
140	
141	Both the **signature** and the eventual **body** for Pin 2 are
142	project-bespoke — Mathlib has the algebraic atom
143	(`Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` in
144	`Mathlib.NumberTheory.RamificationInertia.Basic`) but no scheme-level
145	divisor / morphism-degree wrapper to align with. The iter-178+ planner
146	should:
147	
148	1. Keep the current signature of `morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor` (it
149	   is the right substantive content given the project-bespoke
150	   `Scheme.WeilDivisor`).
151	2. Plan to introduce a private `Scheme.WeilDivisor.pullback` (or a
152	   one-shot pole-divisor construction `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` for
153	   `φ : C ⟶ ℙ¹`) inside `RationalCurveIso.lean` or
154	   `WeilDivisor.lean`.
155	3. Reduce the multiplicativity-of-degree identity through
156	   `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` on a chosen affine cover (the
157	   only point of contact with Mathlib). Budget: ~80-150 LOC for the
158	   pullback + the per-chart Dedekind-domain bridge.
159	
160	No Mathlib **upstream PR** is required — this is genuine new
161	infrastructure that Mathlib lacks, not a re-implementation. If the
162	project ever exports a `Scheme.WeilDivisor.pullback`-style API, it
163	*would* be worth a future Mathlib PR (Stacks tags 02RW, 02RX, 0AX5
164	cover this material), but that is out of scope for the iter-181/182
165	plan.
166	
167	## Possible Mathlib gap-fill (informational, not blocking)
168	
169	For the eventual upstream PR (post-project), the following would close
170	the gap in Mathlib:
171	
172	- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.degree : (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsIntegral X]
173	  [IsIntegral Y] [IsFinite f] [LocallyOfFiniteType f] : ℕ`
174	  defined as `Module.finrank Y.functionField X.functionField`.
175	- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor` (currently project-bespoke).
176	- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.pullback (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsFinite f] :
177	  Y.WeilDivisor →+ X.WeilDivisor`.
178	- `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_pullback` (Hartshorne II.6.9):
179	  `(f^*D).degree = f.degree * D.degree`.
180	
181	The project must not block on any of these — the in-tree route is the
182	correct call for iter-181/182.

304	private noncomputable def localParameterAtInfty (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar]
305	    [IsIntegral (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left] :
306	    { t : (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField // t ≠ 0 } := by
307	  classical
308	  -- The grading on `k̄[X₀, X₁]`.
309	  let 𝒜 : ℕ → Submodule kbar (MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) :=
310	    projectiveLineBarGrading kbar
311	  -- The element `f = X 1` is homogeneous of degree 1.
312	  let f : MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar := MvPolynomial.X 1
313	  have hf : f ∈ 𝒜 1 := MvPolynomial.isHomogeneous_X kbar 1
314	  have hf0 : (MvPolynomial.X 0 : MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) ∈ 𝒜 (1 • 1) := by
315	    simpa using MvPolynomial.isHomogeneous_X kbar 0
316	  -- The ratio coordinate `X 0 / X 1 ∈ Away 𝒜 (X 1)` (degree 0).
317	  let x01 : HomogeneousLocalization.Away 𝒜 f :=
318	    HomogeneousLocalization.Away.mk 𝒜 hf 1 (MvPolynomial.X 0) hf0
319	  -- Basic facts about `X 0` and `X 1`.
320	  have hf_ne : f ≠ 0 := MvPolynomial.X_ne_zero 1
321	  have hX0_ne : (MvPolynomial.X 0 : MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) ≠ 0 :=
322	    MvPolynomial.X_ne_zero 0
323	  -- Step (a): `x01 ≠ 0` in `Away 𝒜 (X 1)`.
324	  have hx01_ne : x01 ≠ 0 := by
325	    intro heq
326	    have hval :
327	        x01.val =
328	          (0 : HomogeneousLocalization.Away 𝒜 f).val :=
329	      congrArg HomogeneousLocalization.val heq
330	    rw [HomogeneousLocalization.val_zero,
331	        HomogeneousLocalization.Away.val_mk] at hval
332	    rw [Localization.mk_eq_mk'_apply,
333	        IsLocalization.mk'_eq_zero_iff] at hval
334	    obtain ⟨c, hc⟩ := hval
335	    rcases c.2 with ⟨n, hcn⟩
336	    have hcv_ne : (c : MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) ≠ 0 := by
337	      simp only [← hcn]
338	      exact pow_ne_zero _ hf_ne
339	    exact hX0_ne ((mul_eq_zero.mp hc).resolve_left hcv_ne)
340	  -- Step (b): `Away 𝒜 (X 1)` is nontrivial (needed for `Nonempty (basicOpen f)`).
341	  haveI hL : Nontrivial (Localization.Away f) :=
342	    (IsLocalization.injective (Localization.Away f)
343	      (powers_le_nonZeroDivisors_of_noZeroDivisors hf_ne)).nontrivial
344	  haveI hAway : Nontrivial (HomogeneousLocalization.Away 𝒜 f) := by
345	    refine ⟨1, 0, ?_⟩
346	    intro heq
347	    have hval :
348	        (1 : HomogeneousLocalization.Away 𝒜 f).val =
349	          (0 : HomogeneousLocalization.Away 𝒜 f).val :=
350	      congrArg HomogeneousLocalization.val heq
351	    rw [HomogeneousLocalization.val_one,
352	        HomogeneousLocalization.val_zero] at hval
353	    exact one_ne_zero hval
354	  -- Step (c): the chart-1 affine open `U = D₊(X 1)` is nonempty (via `awayι`).
355	  let U : (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.Opens := Proj.basicOpen 𝒜 f
356	  haveI hU_nonempty : Nonempty U := by
357	    obtain ⟨p⟩ := (inferInstance :
358	        Nonempty (Spec (CommRingCat.of (HomogeneousLocalization.Away 𝒜 f))))
359	    refine ⟨⟨(Proj.awayι 𝒜 f hf Nat.one_pos).base p, ?_⟩⟩
360	    -- The point lies in the image of `awayι`, which equals `basicOpen 𝒜 f`.
361	    have hrange : (Proj.awayι 𝒜 f hf Nat.one_pos).opensRange = U :=
362	      Proj.opensRange_awayι 𝒜 f hf Nat.one_pos
363	    rw [show U = (Proj.awayι 𝒜 f hf Nat.one_pos).opensRange from hrange.symm]
364	    exact ⟨p, rfl⟩
365	  -- Step (d): build the section in `Γ(Proj 𝒜, U)`.
366	  let sec : Γ((ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left, U) :=
367	    (Proj.basicOpenIsoAway 𝒜 f hf Nat.one_pos).hom.hom x01
368	  -- Step (e): embed in the function field via `germToFunctionField`.
369	  refine ⟨((ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.germToFunctionField U).hom sec, ?_⟩
370	  -- Step (f): non-zero via injectivity.
371	  intro ht
372	  -- `germToFunctionField` is injective on integral schemes.
373	  have hinj_germ :=
374	    Scheme.germToFunctionField_injective (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left U
375	  have hsec_zero : sec = 0 := by
376	    apply hinj_germ
377	    rw [map_zero]
378	    exact ht
379	  -- `basicOpenIsoAway` is an iso, hence its `.hom.hom` is injective.
380	  have hiso_inj :
381	      Function.Injective
382	        (CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.hom
383	          (Proj.basicOpenIsoAway 𝒜 f hf Nat.one_pos).hom) :=
384	    (CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.bijective_of_isIso _).1
385	  apply hx01_ne
386	  apply hiso_inj
387	  change sec = (Proj.basicOpenIsoAway 𝒜 f hf Nat.one_pos).hom.hom 0
388	  rw [hsec_zero, map_zero]
389	  rfl

391	/-! ### Iter-190 → iter-191 Lane I Pin 2 corrective substrate
392	(public `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` + Hartshorne~II.6.9 public pin)
393	
394	Per iter-190 plan-phase Pin 2 corrective Option (a): `Hom.poleDivisor` is
395	defined as `positivePart (principal (algebraMap _ _ t_∞) halg) =
396	(φ^* t_∞)_0 = φ^*[∞]`. The iter-191 refactor lifts the underlying
397	`positivePart` def and the `degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`
398	typed-sorry pin from file-local `private` placeholders to the public
399	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` /
400	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`
401	of `WeilDivisor.lean` (resolving the fully-qualified-name clash that
402	arose when both files declared the same `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart`
403	at link time). The consumer in `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank`
404	below directly applies the public pin specialised at
405	`t = (localParameterAtInfty kbar).val`. -/
406	
407	/-- **The pole divisor of a morphism `φ : C → ℙ¹` of smooth proper curves
408	over `k̄`.** As a Weil divisor on `C`, it is `φ^*[∞]` — the pullback of the
409	divisor `[∞]` on `ℙ¹` along `φ`.
410	
411	**iter-190 substantive body (Pin 2 corrective Option (a) — positive part of
412	principal).** The body is the *positive part* of the principal Weil divisor
413	on `C` of the pulled-back local parameter
414	`algebraMap K(ℙ¹) K(C) (localParameterAtInfty kbar).val`:
415	
416	  `Hom.poleDivisor φ := positivePart (principal (algebraMap K(ℙ¹) K(C) t_∞) _)`
417	
418	Mathematically, `div(φ^* t_∞) = (φ^* t_∞)_0 − (φ^* t_∞)_∞` on `C`, where
419	`(φ^* t_∞)_0 = φ^*[∞]` (the zeros of `φ^* t_∞` are exactly the preimages
420	of `∞`, where `t_∞` vanishes on `ℙ¹`) and `(φ^* t_∞)_∞ = φ^*[0]` (the
421	poles are the preimages of `0`). Extracting the positive part of
422	`div(φ^* t_∞)` exactly recovers `φ^*[∞]` on the nose.
423	
424	**Why the positive-part wrap is essential (Pin 2 structural-conflict
425	diagnosis, iter-189 → iter-190 corrective).** The naked principal divisor
426	`principal (algebraMap _ _ t_∞) halg` has degree zero on a complete
427	nonsingular curve (Hartshorne II.6.10 / Stacks 0BE3,
428	`Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero` of `WeilDivisor.lean`),
429	whereas the RHS `Module.finrank K(ℙ¹) K(C)` of
430	`Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` is positive (= deg φ ≥ 1) at every
431	call site that uses the φ-induced algebra structure. The positive-part
432	wrap restores provability: `degree (positivePart (principal (φ^# t_∞)))
433	= degree (φ^*[∞]) = deg(φ) = [K(C):K(ℙ¹)]` (Hartshorne~II.6.9).
434	
435	The φ-dependence is carried by the `[Algebra K(ℙ¹) K(C)]` instance
436	binder, which at every call site is the canonical `φ`-induced
437	function-field map (per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md` Decision 2).
438	Changing the algebra instance changes the divisor; in particular, the
439	unfolding
440	`Hom.poleDivisor φ ⇝ positivePart (principal (algebraMap _ _ t) _)`
441	is visible to definitional unfolding, *not* opaque behind
442	`Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / empty `proof_wanted` placeholders.
443	
444	**Helper budget**: 1 named typed sorry (`localParameterAtInfty`, the
445	local-parameter choice; substrate gap on the chart-`1` ratio coordinate
446	of `projectiveLineBarAffineCover`). The Pin 2 corrective additionally
447	introduces a file-local `WeilDivisor.positivePart` def + named typed-sorry
448	pin `WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_localParameterAtInfty_eq_finrank`
449	above; these are scheduled to migrate to the public
450	`Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` /
451	`Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` of
452	`WeilDivisor.lean` once the parallel iter-190 WeilDivisor prover lands
453	the public versions.
454	
455	Blueprint reference: `def:Hom.poleDivisor` (Hartshorne IV §2 opening +
456	II §6.9; chapter `chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso` §2 "Convention on
457	the pole divisor"); iter-190 plan-phase Pin 2 corrective NOTE on
458	`lem:degree_via_pole_divisor`. -/
459	noncomputable def Hom.poleDivisor
460	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
461	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
462	    [IsProper C.hom] [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
463	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
464	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
465	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
466	    [IsIntegral (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left]
467	    [Algebra (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField C.left.functionField]
468	    (_φ : C ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar kbar) :
469	    C.left.WeilDivisor :=
470	  -- Pull a chosen local parameter `t_∞ ∈ K(ℙ¹)` back to `K(C)` along the
471	  -- canonical `φ`-induced algebra map, take its principal divisor, then
472	  -- extract the positive part (i.e. the zeros, which on a complete curve
473	  -- coincide with `φ^*[∞]`). The `t_∞ ≠ 0` ⟹ `algebraMap _ _ t_∞ ≠ 0`
474	  -- step is automatic from injectivity of the algebra map
475	  -- `K(ℙ¹) → K(C)` (a ring hom out of a field is injective).
476	  let t := localParameterAtInfty kbar
477	  have halg :
478	      algebraMap (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField
479	          C.left.functionField t.val ≠ 0 := by
480	    intro h
481	    apply t.property
482	    have hinj :
483	        Function.Injective
484	          (algebraMap (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField
485	            C.left.functionField) :=
486	      RingHom.injective _
487	    have h0 :
488	        algebraMap (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField
489	            C.left.functionField 0 = 0 :=
490	      RingHom.map_zero _
491	    exact hinj (h.trans h0.symm)
492	  WeilDivisor.positivePart
493	    (Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal
494	      (algebraMap (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField
495	        C.left.functionField t.val)
496	      halg)
497	
498	/-- **Degree identity for the pole divisor of a non-constant `C ⟶ ℙ¹`**
499	(Hartshorne IV §2 p. 299 opening + II §6.9 multiplicativity of degree under
500	finite pullback). The Weil-divisor degree of the pole divisor
501	`Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor φ = φ^*[∞]` equals the function-field-extension
502	degree `[K(C) : k̄(ℙ¹)] = Module.finrank K(ℙ¹) K(C)`, where the
503	`K(ℙ¹)`-algebra structure on `K(C)` is the canonical `φ`-induced one (the
504	typeclass binder pins this; per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md` Decision 2
505	convention).
506	
507	**Tier-3 honest sorry (iter-183).** The substantive body is iter-184+ work
508	via the affine-chart-localised `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia`
509	(Stacks `02RW` / `0AX5`) per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin2.md` Decision 2.
510	The strategy: pick an affine open `Spec A ⊂ ℙ¹` containing `∞`; the
511	preimage `Spec B ⊂ C` is finite over `Spec A` (non-constancy + smooth
512	proper curves ⟹ finite); both `A → B` are Dedekind extensions and
513	`Σ_{Q above P} e(Q|P) · f(Q|P) = [Frac B : Frac A] = [K(C) : K(ℙ¹)]`
514	identifies the pole-divisor degree with the function-field degree. The
515	non-constancy hypothesis is consumed in the finiteness reduction.
516	
517	This helper is the **single substantive gap** of
518	`morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor` after the iter-182 plan-phase
519	signature strengthening: the wrapper body below is sorry-free assembly
520	that reduces to this helper. -/
521	theorem Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank
522	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
523	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper C.hom]
524	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
525	    [IsIntegral C.left] [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
526	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
527	    [IsIntegral (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left]
528	    (φ : C ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar kbar)
529	    (_hφ_non_const :
530	      ∀ Q : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar kbar,
531	        φ ≠ toUnit C ≫ Q)
532	    [Algebra (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField C.left.functionField]
533	    [Module.Finite (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField
534	      C.left.functionField] :
535	    Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree (Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor φ) =
536	      (Module.finrank
537	        (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField
538	        C.left.functionField : ℤ) := by
539	  -- **iter-190 Pin 2 corrective Option (a) closure** + **iter-191 public-pin
540	  -- migration** (per iter-191 plan-phase Lane I `lane-i-positivepart-clash-fix`
541	  -- directive resolving the duplicate fully-qualified-name clash).
542	  --
543	  -- The iter-187/iter-189 structural conflict was: the body of `Hom.poleDivisor`
544	  -- was `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal (algebraMap _ _ t_∞) halg`, whose degree
545	  -- is zero on a complete nonsingular curve (`principal_degree_zero` of
546	  -- `WeilDivisor.lean`) while the RHS `Module.finrank K(ℙ¹) K(C)` is positive
547	  -- (= deg φ ≥ 1) at every call site with the φ-induced algebra structure.
548	  -- The iter-190 corrective wraps the principal divisor in a positive-part
549	  -- operator, recovering `φ^*[∞]` on the nose:
550	  --   `Hom.poleDivisor φ
551	  --     = Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart (Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal
552	  --         (algebraMap _ _ (localParameterAtInfty kbar).val) halg)`.
553	  -- Under this corrected body the theorem reduces (by `unfold`) to the public
554	  -- Hartshorne~II.6.9 pin
555	  -- `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` of
556	  -- `WeilDivisor.lean` (iter-191+ body via the affine-chart
557	  -- `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` chain per
558	  -- `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin2.md` Decision 2), specialised at
559	  -- `t = (localParameterAtInfty kbar).val`.
560	  unfold Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor
561	  exact Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank
562	    C (localParameterAtInfty kbar).val _
563	
564	/-- **Degree of a non-constant morphism to `ℙ¹` via its pole divisor**
565	(Hartshorne IV §2 p. 299 opening + II §6.9 multiplicativity of degree
566	under finite pullback).
567	
568	For a non-constant morphism `φ : C ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar k̄` from a smooth
569	proper geometrically irreducible curve `C` over an algebraically closed
570	field `k̄`, there exists a positive integer `d` (= `deg(φ)`) together
571	with a Weil divisor `D` on `C` (= the pole divisor `φ^*[∞]` of the
572	pulled-back affine coordinate) of total degree `d`.
573	
574	The non-constancy hypothesis is encoded as `∀ Q, φ ≠ toUnit C ≫ Q`
575	(no constant morphism `C ⟶ ℙ¹` agrees with `φ`); this is equivalent to
576	`φ` being surjective onto `ℙ¹_{k̄}` since the image is a closed
577	irreducible subset of `ℙ¹_{k̄}` of dimension ≥ 1, and the only such is
578	`ℙ¹_{k̄}` itself.
579	
580	iter-178+ body: a non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves
581	over `k̄` is automatically finite (proper + quasi-finite). The
582	function-field extension `k̄(ℙ¹) ↪ K(C)` is finite of some degree
583	`d = [K(C) : k̄(ℙ¹)] ≥ 1`. Take `D := φ^*[∞] ∈ Div(C)` via the standard
584	"pullback of a Weil divisor along a finite morphism" operation; by
585	Hartshorne II.6.9, `deg(D) = deg(φ) · deg([∞]) = d · 1 = d`. The
586	positivity `d ≥ 1` follows from non-constancy (else `deg(φ) = 0` would
587	force `φ` constant). Both the finite-pullback operation and the
588	multiplicativity-of-degree identity are project-bespoke sub-lemmas
589	threaded inside the iter-178+ body (cf. the closely related
590	`Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero` of `RR.1`).
591	
592	Blueprint reference: `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` (Hartshorne IV §2
593	opening, p. 299; II §6.9 p. 137 for multiplicativity).
594	
595	**Signature strengthening (iter-182 Pin 2).** The iter-177 file-skeleton
596	signature output existential `∃ d D, 0 < d ∧ D.degree = (d : ℤ)` was
597	flagged `weakened-wrong` by the iter-181 `lean-vs-blueprint-checker`:
598	the existential is discharged by ANY positive-degree divisor on `C` and
599	does not reference `φ`. iter-182 strengthens the output to pin `D` to
600	`Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor φ` (a *specific* divisor depending on `φ`) and
601	pin its degree to `Module.finrank (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField
602	C.left.functionField` (the function-field-extension degree `[K(C) : k̄(ℙ¹)]`).
603	The `[Algebra ...]` instance binder pins the intended `Algebra` structure
604	to be the canonical `φ`-induced function-field map (matching the iter-181
605	Pin 3 sig refinement convention). See `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin2.md`
606	(verdict PROCEED) for the body recipe via
607	`Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia`. -/

568	/-! ## §7. Linear equivalence and the divisor class group -/
569	
570	/-- **Linear equivalence of Weil divisors.**
571	
572	Two Weil divisors `D, D' ∈ Div(X)` are linearly equivalent, written `D ~ D'`, if
573	and only if there exists a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` with
574	`D - D' = div(f) ∈ Div(X)`.
575	
576	`~` is an equivalence relation (reflexivity from `div(1) = 0`, symmetry from
577	`div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, transitivity from `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, all via
578	`thm:principal_hom`). The quotient `Cl(X) := Div(X) / im(div)` is the
579	**divisor class group** of `X`.

431	For every nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×` on a smooth proper curve `C`

442	(finite morphism induced by a non-constant rational function; multiplicativity
443	of degree under finite pullback, Hartshorne II.6.9) are deferred to follow-up
444	iters of `RR.1`. -/
